_meta.description: Unified music knowledge — genres, artists, production reference

tiers.profile: Prose-derived from research (no drum/bass params yet)

classic_house.name: Classic House / Old School House
classic_house.description: Classic house is the golden era sound—1986 to 1992—before the genre splintered into subgenres. This is the canonical blueprint: four-on-the-floor kick, claps snapping on 2 and 4, hi-hats swinging with disco's ghost still in the machine. Born in Chicago's Warehouse and refined in New York's Paradise Garage, this sound carries disco's DNA but stripped to essentials and rebuilt with drum machines. The 909 or 808 provides the pulse, but the feel is human—swing pushed to 55-65% transforms rigid 16ths into something that breathes and bounces. The kick is warm and present but never dominates; it's a foundation for the piano and vocals to shine. Tempo sits in the 120-124 sweet spot, fast enough to move bodies but slow enough for the groove to stretch out and the emotion to land.
classic_house.production: The piano is the defining voice of classic house—rolling chords, often in major keys, played with gospel fervor. Think the opening of 'Promised Land' or the breakdown in 'French Kiss'. Rhodes and organ add warmth, strings swell for emotion, and when the vocal comes in, it *preaches*. This is soulful music, spiritual even—Joe Smooth's 'Promised Land' is literally a hymn. The bassline walks and moves, often an octave pattern with passing notes, locked tight with the kick but with its own melodic identity. The mix is warm, even muddy by modern standards—tape saturation, room reverb, the imperfections of early sampling all contribute to that vintage feel. Vocal samples from disco and soul records chop and loop, 'work it' and 'feel the music' becoming mantras. The breakdown is sacred: drums drop out, the piano or strings carry the weight, the crowd raises their hands, and when the kick returns it's resurrection. This is church music for the dancefloor.

bass.style: walking octave pattern, melodic movement

references: Promised Land - Joe Smooth
French Kiss - Lil Louis
Can You Party - Royal House
Ride on Time - Black Box
Pacific State - 808 State
Good Life - Inner City

detroit_techno.name: Detroit Techno
detroit_techno.description: Detroit techno emerged in the mid-80s from Belleville Three (Juan Atkins, Derrick May, Kevin Saunderson), fusing Kraftwerk's precision with Parliament-Funkadelic's soul. The sound is mechanical yet emotional—cold synthesizers carrying warm melodies. The kick is punchy but not overwhelming, usually a tight 909 with moderate decay around 0.3-0.4, tuned neutral or slightly up. Snares crack cleanly on 2 and 4, often with a short reverb tail. Hi-hats drive the track with steady 16ths, closed hats dominant with open hats accenting the offbeats sparingly.
detroit_techno.production: The bassline is the soul—deep, melodic, often a sawtooth or square wave filtered to sit warm in the low-mids. Detroit bass moves, it's not a static root note. Chord stabs punctuate with string-like pads or Rhodes-style keys, heavy on the minor 7ths and 9ths for that bittersweet futurism. The mix is spacious—every element has room to breathe. Reverb is used tastefully, never washing things out. The feel is driving but sophisticated, like a machine dreaming of being human.

bass.style: melodic, moving lines

references: Strings of Life - Derrick May
No UFOs - Juan Atkins
Big Fun - Inner City

berlin_techno.name: Berlin Techno
berlin_techno.description: Berlin techno is the sound of concrete and strobe lights, born in the reunification-era clubs like Tresor and Berghain. Where Detroit dreams, Berlin pounds. The kick is the center of everything—deep, heavy, with a long tail that dominates the low end. Tuned down (-3 to -6 on a 909) for maximum chest impact, decay pushed to 0.5-0.7 so it breathes and pulses. The tempo runs hotter than Detroit, 130-138 being the sweet spot for that relentless forward momentum.
berlin_techno.production: Hi-hats are minimal—often just a sparse pattern or filtered noise. The snare or clap hits hard but dry, no reverb, creating a stark industrial feel. Bass is often just the kick tail itself, or a simple sub-bass drone following the root note. When synth lines appear, they're hypnotic single-note sequences, often with heavy delay creating rhythmic echoes. The Berlin aesthetic is reduction—strip away everything unnecessary until only the essential groove remains. Space and silence are instruments. The DJ EQ is part of the composition—tracks are designed to have their lows and highs manipulated live.

bass.style: minimal, often just kick tail

references: Spastik - Plastikman
Acid Phase - Emmanuel Top
The Bells - Jeff Mills

industrial_techno.name: Industrial Techno
industrial_techno.description: Industrial techno takes Berlin's foundation and feeds it through a metal grinder. Pioneered by labels like Mord, Perc Trax, and Semantica, this is techno for abandoned factories and dystopian futures. The kick is distorted, overdriven, sometimes layered with noise bursts. Where Berlin techno breathes, industrial suffocates—the compression is heavy, the mix is dense and aggressive. Tempos push higher, 135-145, with an unrelenting mechanical precision that feels inhuman.
industrial_techno.production: Percussion is weaponized—industrial samples, metallic clangs, reversed crashes, and noise sweeps create a hostile sonic environment. The 909's rimshot becomes a whip crack at high velocity, the toms are tuned into metallic thuds. Bass is distorted and mean, often with bitcrushing or waveshaping to add grit. Synth lines when present are harsh—buzzing, screaming, teeth-on-edge frequencies. The overall mix is lo-fi by design, with intentional clipping and saturation. This isn't music for dancing—it's music for exorcism. The swing is zero, everything locked to a merciless grid.

bass.style: distorted, aggressive

references: Look What Your Love Has Done To Me - Perc
Leather - Paula Temple
Function - Vatican Shadow

chicago_house.name: Chicago House
chicago_house.description: Chicago house is the original—the blueprint written in the Warehouse and the Music Box by Frankie Knuckles, Ron Hardy, and the Hot Mix 5. It's joyful, soulful, built on disco's bones but stripped to essentials. The 909 kick hits four-on-the-floor but lighter than techno, decay around 0.3-0.4 for a tighter punch that leaves room for the bass. The clap is queen here—snappy, present, often with a touch of reverb to give it body. The closed hi-hat swings hard, 60-70% swing turning mechanical 16ths into human groove.
chicago_house.production: The bass is where Chicago lives—fat 303 lines or Juno bass with movement and soul. Not just root notes but walking patterns, octave jumps, slides that reach and yearn. Piano stabs punctuate with jazzy chords, organ swells lift the spirit, vocal samples preach and testify. The mix is warm, even muddy by modern standards—this is music made on tape, meant for sweaty rooms with big speakers. Reverb is generous, creating space and depth. The swing is essential—straight Chicago house doesn't exist. That shuffle is the difference between programmed and played, between machine and human.

bass.style: walking, melodic, soulful

references: Your Love - Frankie Knuckles
Can You Feel It - Mr. Fingers
Move Your Body - Marshall Jefferson

deep_house.name: Deep House
deep_house.description: Deep house is Chicago's introspective cousin—late night music for coming down, for 4am conversations, for souls seeking warmth in the dark. Larry Heard's 'Can You Feel It' is the template: pads that float like clouds, bass that pulses like a heartbeat, drums that suggest rather than demand. The kick is soft, round, often with the attack slightly smoothed. The tempo drops to 120-122 for that hypnotic, almost metronomic pulse. Minor keys dominate, but not sad—contemplative, intimate.
deep_house.production: Pads are essential—lush, evolving, often Juno-style strings with slow attacks and long releases that blur into each other. The bass is simpler than Chicago house, often holding notes longer, letting them breathe and resonate. Rhodes and electric piano provide harmonic movement with jazzy 7th and 9th chords. The hi-hat pattern is often simplified, sometimes just 8ths instead of 16ths, creating more space. Reverb is long and deep, creating a sense of vast interior space. Vocals when present are buried in the mix, more texture than message. The overall feel is submerged, underwater—sound moving through liquid rather than air.

bass.style: sustained, pulsing, minimal movement

references: Can You Feel It - Mr. Fingers
Mystery of Love - Larry Heard
Strings of Life (Derrick May remix)

tech_house.name: Tech House
tech_house.description: Tech house is the practical middle ground—house's groove meets techno's drive. Emerging in the mid-90s from UK producers like Mr. C and Eddie Richards, it strips away house's soulful excess and techno's industrial severity. The kick is present and defined, tuned fairly neutral, with moderate decay. The groove is still there but less pronounced than Chicago—swing around 25-35%, enough to feel human without being loose. Tempo sits in the 125-128 sweet spot, equally comfortable warming up a room or keeping it moving.
tech_house.production: The hi-hat pattern is busier than deep house but more restrained than Chicago—often 16ths with velocity variation creating movement without chaos. Percussion is key: congas, bongos, shakers add organic texture over the electronic foundation. The bass is functional—a solid, repetitive line that locks with the kick and doesn't try to be melodic. Synth stabs are short and percussive rather than atmospheric. The mix is clean and modern, prioritizing clarity over warmth. Vocals are usually cut-up samples or single phrases repeated for hypnotic effect. This is DJ tool music—designed for mixing, building, and maintaining energy without demanding attention.

bass.style: functional, locked to kick

references: Lizard - Mauro Picotto
Finally - Kings of Tomorrow
Cafe Del Mar - Energy 52

acid_house.name: Acid House
acid_house.description: Acid house was an accident that changed everything. In 1987, DJ Pierre twisted the knobs of a $300 TB-303 bass synthesizer and discovered the squelch—that wet, resonant, filter-swept sound that would launch a thousand raves. The 303 was designed to replace bass guitars; instead it became an alien instrument making sounds no bassist could imagine. The acid sound is all about the filter: high resonance creating sharp peaks, envelope modulation making each note bloom and decay, slides connecting notes with liquid portamento.
acid_house.production: The drum pattern is classic Chicago—four-on-the-floor kick, claps on 2 and 4, swinging hi-hats. But the 303 is the star. The pattern is usually simple, often just a few notes, because the timbral variation carries the interest. Accent certain notes for emphasis, slide between others for that reaching, yearning quality. The cutoff sweeps are the hook—slowly opening the filter as the track builds, closing it down for tension. Resonance at 60-80% creates that characteristic scream. The rest of the track stays minimal to let the acid line dominate. This sound defined the Second Summer of Love and still sounds like the future.

bass.style: 303 acid line with slides and accents

references: Acid Tracks - Phuture
Voodoo Ray - A Guy Called Gerald
Pacific State - 808 State

acid_techno.name: Acid Techno
acid_techno.description: Acid techno takes the 303 out of the warehouse and into the rave—faster, harder, more relentless. Where acid house seduces, acid techno attacks. Born in the early 90s UK free party scene and German clubs, this is 303 pushed to its limits: screaming resonance, rapid-fire patterns, filter sweeps that feel like sonic assault. The tempo jumps to 140+ BPM, the kick gets harder, and the acid line becomes a weapon rather than a voice.
acid_techno.production: The drums hit harder than house—closer to Berlin techno's weight but with more hi-hat energy. The 303 pattern is often busier, 16th notes instead of 8ths, with frequent accents creating a stuttering, aggressive feel. Slides are used constantly, notes bending into each other in sheets of resonant noise. The cutoff modulation is extreme—full sweeps from closed to screaming. Multiple 303 lines sometimes layer, creating walls of acid. The overall aesthetic is psychedelic in the true sense: overwhelming, reality-dissolving, designed for altered states. No subtlety, no restraint—just pure acid energy.

bass.style: aggressive, rapid 16th patterns

references: Mentasm - Second Phase
Dominator - Human Resource
Acid Eiffel - Hardfloor

electro.name: Electro
electro.description: Electro is the original electronic street music—born from hip-hop, raised on Kraftwerk, defined by the TR-808. Afrika Bambaataa's 'Planet Rock' in 1982 fused German electronics with Bronx attitude and created a new form. The 808 is essential: that deep, booming kick that rattles trunks, the snappy electronic snare, the metallic cowbell that became electro's signature. The tempo is slower than house or techno, usually 110-120, giving room for the funk to breathe.
electro.production: The rhythm is syncopated and funky—not four-on-the-floor but broken patterns with kicks landing on the one and off-beats. The snare typically cracks on two and four but with ghost notes and rolls adding complexity. The cowbell drives the track, often on every quarter note or in syncopated patterns. Claps layer with snares for thickness. The bass is huge and subby, often simple octave patterns but tuned to shake foundations. Synth leads are robotic and melodic—often monophonic arpeggios or single-note hooks. Vocoder vocals add to the mechanical human hybrid aesthetic. The feel is futuristic retro—what 1982 thought 2000 would sound like.

bass.style: huge sub bass, simple patterns

references: Planet Rock - Afrika Bambaataa
Clear - Cybotron
Rockit - Herbie Hancock

drum_and_bass.name: Drum and Bass
drum_and_bass.description: Drum and bass is breakbeat science taken to the extreme—the sound of jungle rhythms time-stretched and reassembled at 170 BPM. Emerging from the UK rave scene in the early 90s, it took hip-hop's breakbeats, sped them up, and added crushing sub bass. The snare hits on 2 and 4 but the kick pattern is complex, syncopated, often following the original break's ghost notes. The tempo feels impossibly fast but the half-time sub bass grounds everything, creating a push-pull between frantic tops and heavy lows.
drum_and_bass.production: The break is everything—usually the Amen, Apache, or Think break chopped and rearranged into new patterns. Programming these from scratch requires understanding the original breaks: kicks that land between beats, snares with subtle timing variations, ghost notes that create the rolling feel. The sub bass is a sine wave or filtered square hitting hard on the one, often with pitch bends and slides. It's tuned to specific keys and carefully controlled to avoid muddiness. Higher synth elements—reece basses, pads, strings—fill the mid-range. The mix is split: all the break energy lives above 100Hz, all the weight below it. This separation is crucial.

bass.style: sub bass with pitch bends, half-time feel

references: Inner City Life - Goldie
Super Sharp Shooter - DJ Zinc
Brown Paper Bag - Roni Size

jungle.name: Jungle
jungle.description: Jungle is drum and bass's rawer, more soulful ancestor—before the genre got streamlined, when the breaks were chopped rougher and the samples pulled from reggae, ragga, and soul. The Amen break is king here, timestretched and pitch-shifted into new forms, the snare hits crunchy and distorted rather than clean. The tempo is slightly slower than modern D&B, giving more room for the ragga vocals and dub influences. This is sound system music—designed for massive bass bins.
jungle.production: The breaks are treated more aggressively—timestretching creates artifacts that become part of the sound, giving that characteristic crunchy, gritty texture. Snare rolls are constant, creating the 'rollers' feel that defines the genre. The bass is often a reese—a detuned sawtooth creating that growling, moving texture that became jungle's signature. Samples from reggae and dancehall tracks are chopped and looped, often pitching vocals up to create the 'chipmunk' effect. Heavy use of delay and reverb on snares creates space and depth. The overall mix is muddier than modern D&B, warmer, more chaotic—sound system music that prioritizes impact over clarity.

bass.style: reese bass, detuned and growling

references: Original Nuttah - Shy FX
Pulp Fiction - Alex Reece
Incredible - M Beat

trance.name: Trance
trance.description: Trance is electronic music as transcendence—designed to induce altered states through repetition, build, and release. Born in German clubs in the early 90s, it drew from techno's drive but added emotional melodies and epic structures. The kick is prominent but cleaner than techno, tuned fairly neutral with moderate decay. The tempo runs 138-142 typically, fast enough to be propulsive but not overwhelming. Everything serves the build: layers adding gradually, filters opening, tension accumulating until the breakdown releases it all.
trance.production: The structure is crucial: long intros building elements one by one, breakdowns stripping everything back to pads and atmosphere, drops returning with full force. Synth leads are bright and emotional—supersaw patches with multiple detuned oscillators creating that huge, shimmering sound. Arpeggios run constantly, often gated to the tempo for a pulsing effect. Pads are massive, occupying the entire stereo field with slow-moving chord progressions. The bassline is simple and driving, usually following the root note with octave variation. Reverb and delay are used extensively to create space and psychedelic atmosphere. The emotional manipulation is deliberate—trance is designed to make you feel.

bass.style: driving, root-note focused with octaves

references: Age of Love - Age of Love
Children - Robert Miles
For An Angel - Paul van Dyk

minimal_techno.name: Minimal Techno
minimal_techno.description: Minimal techno is subtraction as art—taking the elements of techno and asking how few are truly needed. Pioneered by Richie Hawtin, Ricardo Villalobos, and the Kompakt label in the late 90s, it replaced big builds with micro-variations, bombast with hypnosis. The kick is often understated, sometimes barely there—more click than thump. The tempo drops to 125 range, slower than Berlin, allowing each element room to breathe and shift. Tracks evolve over 8-10 minutes through tiny, almost imperceptible changes.
minimal_techno.production: Percussion is textural rather than rhythmic—clicks, blips, filtered noise, organic sounds processed beyond recognition. The hi-hat might be a sliver of white noise, the snare a filtered snap. Elements drift in and out, appearing for 32 bars then vanishing. The bass is often just a sub presence, felt more than heard, pulsing on and off-beats to create movement. When melodic elements appear, they're fragments—a single note repeating with slight filter modulation, a chord appearing once every 16 bars. The mix is pristine and wide, every element placed precisely in the stereo field. This is headphone music as much as club music—details reveal themselves on close listening.

bass.style: subtle sub presence, minimal movement

references: Spastik - Plastikman
Fizheuer Zull - Ricardo Villalobos
Der Klang Der Familie - Various

breakbeat.name: Breakbeat
breakbeat.description: Breakbeat is the broad church of broken rhythm—any electronic music that doesn't hit four-on-the-floor. The term covers everything from big beat to breaks to nu-skool, but the unifying element is that syncopated, hip-hop-derived kick pattern. The Chemical Brothers and Fatboy Slim brought this to stadiums in the 90s, but the roots go back to electro and hip-hop. The kick lands on the one and off-beats, creating space and bounce that four-on-the-floor can't touch.
breakbeat.production: The break pattern is usually based on funk breaks—James Brown's drummers, the Winstons, etc.—programmed or sampled and processed. The kick sits heavy on one, often with a second hit around beat 3 or 3.5 creating the signature bounce. Snares crack on two and four with attitude. The energy comes from the interplay between kick and snare rather than the steady pulse of house or techno. Bass lines have room to move and groove, often with funkier patterns than four-on-the-floor allows. Synths can be massive—big acid lines, distorted leads, filtered stabs. The aesthetic is more rock than house, more attitude than subtlety. This is music for fist-pumping.

bass.style: funky, syncopated, room to move

references: Block Rockin Beats - Chemical Brothers
Right Here Right Now - Fatboy Slim
Firestarter - Prodigy

ambient.name: Ambient
ambient.description: Ambient is music as environment—Brian Eno's original definition was 'as ignorable as it is interesting.' Emerging from Eno's experiments in the 70s and evolving through the chill-out rooms of 90s raves, ambient strips away rhythm in favor of texture and space. There may be no drums at all, or just subtle pulses deep in the mix. The tempo, when present, is slow—60-80 BPM—creating a sense of floating rather than moving. This is music for states between waking and sleeping.
ambient.production: Pads are the primary instrument—long, evolving textures created through layered synthesizers with slow attacks and infinite releases. Reverb is massive, often with decay times of 10+ seconds, creating cavernous virtual spaces. Delay feeds back on itself, creating cascading echoes. Field recordings—rain, wind, room tone—blend with synthesized tones. When melodic elements appear, they're sparse and contemplative, single notes or simple intervals rather than progressions. The mix is wide and deep, instruments placed at various distances in the stereo field. Dynamic range is preserved—whisper quiet passages and moments of relative intensity. This is music that rewards patience.

bass.style: drones, long sustained notes

references: Music For Airports - Brian Eno
Selected Ambient Works Vol II - Aphex Twin
Lifeforms - Future Sound of London

idm.name: IDM
idm.description: IDM—Intelligent Dance Music—is a problematic name for a real phenomenon: electronic music that prioritizes complexity and experimentation over danceability. Warp Records artists like Aphex Twin, Autechre, and Boards of Canada defined the sound in the 90s: glitchy, fractured beats, unexpected sound design, structures that confound expectations. The tempo varies wildly—from ambient crawl to drill'n'bass frenzy. The 'intelligent' part isn't about being smarter than other dance music; it's about being too weird for the dancefloor.
idm.production: Drums are often the focus of experimentation—timestretching, granular synthesis, probability-based sequencing creating patterns that never quite repeat. The classic 909 or 808 sounds might appear but processed beyond recognition, layered with glitches and artifacts. Bass can be massive (Aphex Twin's drill bass) or absent entirely. Melody often comes from unexpected sources—detuned synths, mangled samples, sounds that sit ambiguously between tone and noise. The mix might be pristine or deliberately lo-fi. Structure defies verse-chorus conventions—tracks might build for 8 minutes or change completely every 30 seconds. The aesthetic is exploration—following sounds wherever they lead regardless of convention.

bass.style: experimental, processed, unpredictable

references: Windowlicker - Aphex Twin
Roygbiv - Boards of Canada
Gantz Graf - Autechre
idm.lineage: IDM emerged in the early 1990s as a cerebral reaction to the increasingly formulaic nature of mainstream dance music. The term itself is somewhat controversial—many of its practitioners reject the "intelligent" designation as pretentious, preferring "experimental electronic music" or "braindance."
idm.currentScene:
### Active Communities
r/idm - 50k+ members discussing new releases and techniques
IDMf Forums - Technical production discussions and sample sharing
Discogs Groups - Rare release trading and discography completionism
Bandcamp Scene - Self-released artists pushing genre boundaries

doomcore.name: Doomcore
doomcore.description: Doomcore is hardcore techno's slowest, heaviest, most suffocating subgenre — the sound of absolute dread rendered in bass frequencies. Emerging in the mid-to-late 1990s from the Dutch and German hardcore scenes, doomcore strips gabber's speed and replaces it with crushing weight. Where gabber runs at 160-200+ BPM and thrives on manic energy, doomcore deliberately slows down to 130-150 BPM (sometimes even lower, into the 120s), weaponizing the space between kicks to build unbearable tension. The genre was pioneered by artists like The Mover (Marc Acardipane), Disciples of Annihilation, Taciturne, and labels like PCP (Planet Core Productions), Bloody Fist Records (Australia), and Napalm Records. Marc Acardipane is the godfather — his work as The Mover, particularly the 'Frontal Sickness' and 'Astral Demons' releases, defined the blueprint: impossibly heavy kicks, apocalyptic atmospheres, cinematic horror sampling, and a pace that feels like being slowly crushed. The cultural context is important — this emerged from the same Rotterdam/Frankfurt hardcore underground that produced gabber, but doomcore appealed to the darker, more introspective fringe. If gabber is a riot, doomcore is a funeral march through a burning city. The genre cross-pollinates with dark ambient, industrial, and even doom metal in philosophy if not in sound — the shared commitment to slowness as heaviness, to patience as a form of violence. Doomcore events are rare and intense, often in the darkest rooms of hardcore festivals like Thunderdome or Dominator, or in dedicated underground events.
doomcore.production: The kick is everything in doomcore — and it's unlike any other genre's kick. It's massively distorted, with extreme low-end extension, a long sustaining body (decay 0.7-0.9), and often a tonal pitch that drops or sweeps during the hit, creating a sense of impending doom with each strike. Think of it as a 909 kick pitched down -6 to -10 semitones, then run through heavy distortion (tube saturation or waveshaping), with an amp envelope that sustains much longer than any techno kick. The kick IS the bassline in most doomcore — there's no separate bass synth needed because the kick's tail occupies the entire sub region. Between kicks, the space is filled with dark atmospheric pads — often minor-key drones using detuned sawtooth or wavetable oscillators with heavy reverb (5-10 second decay times), pitched low and moving glacially. Horror film samples are staple material: dialogue from movies like Hellraiser, Event Horizon, or obscure 70s horror, processed with reverb and delay to become part of the atmosphere. The hi-hat pattern is minimal or absent entirely — when present, it's a slow, sparse pattern rather than constant 16ths, often a ride cymbal or metallic texture rather than a crisp hat. Snares/claps hit with massive reverb tails, placed sparsely. The arrangement builds tension through subtraction and addition of atmospheric layers rather than through rhythmic complexity. Tracks are long (6-10 minutes), slow-building, and often feature extended ambient passages before the kicks even enter. Distortion is applied liberally to everything — not the clean distortion of modern production but the ugly, grimy, lo-fi saturation of cheap hardware pushed past its limits. Many classic doomcore tracks were produced on hardware like the Roland TR-909, Juno-106, and various rack effects, often deliberately using low sample rates (12-bit or less) for that gritty, crushed quality.

kick.note: Half-time feel at 140 BPM. Each kick sustains long enough to nearly touch the next. Pitched extremely low, heavily distorted. The kick IS the bass.

snare.note: Sparse, often only once per bar or absent entirely. When present, drenched in cavernous reverb. Sometimes replaced by a metallic industrial hit.

clap.note: Optional. When used, heavily reverbed, placed to reinforce the half-time or to create call-and-response with the kick.

ch.note: Extremely sparse. Often just two hits per bar or none at all. Can be replaced with metallic textures or filtered noise bursts.

ride.note: Dark ride cymbal, low-passed, providing minimal time-keeping. Optional — many doomcore tracks have only kick and atmosphere.

bass.style: The kick tail IS the bass. When a separate bass element exists, it's a sub-frequency drone on the root note, sustained and ominous, often with slow pitch modulation or detuning.
bass.note: Cutoff extremely low — sub-only territory. The distorted kick handles all mid-bass content. Any additional bass is felt, not heard.

targets: pad filter cutoff
reverb wet/dry
kick pitch sweep
atmospheric panning
delay feedback
modulation.envelopes: Long attack times (500ms-2s) on pads and atmospheres. Kick envelope has fast attack but extremely long decay/release (300-600ms). Everything moves slowly — the modulation creates the sense of an environment breathing rather than a machine pumping.

mixing.loudness: Heavily compressed and limited, but not for loudness — for density. The kick should feel like it's compressing the entire mix when it hits. Sidechain everything to the kick with a slow release so the mix 'breathes' with each impact. Target -6 to -3 LUFS — this should be oppressively loud.
mixing.stereoWidth: Narrow center (kick mono, sub mono), wide sides for atmospheres and reverb tails. The pads and horror samples spread wide while the kick anchors dead center. Creates a claustrophobic center with vast, haunted space around it.
mixing.eqEmphasis: Massive sub emphasis (30-60Hz) from the kick. Scoop the mids (200-800Hz) to create emptiness and dread. Dark high end — low-pass the master around 12-14kHz to remove brightness and air. The overall tonal balance should feel suffocating and bottom-heavy.
mixing.compression: Bus compression with slow attack (30ms) lets kick transient through, then clamps down. Long release (300ms+) creates pumping. Parallel distortion on the drum bus. Reverb sends compressed separately to keep tails dense. The master chain: EQ (high cut at 14kHz, low shelf boost at 50Hz), compressor (2:1, slow), soft clipper, limiter.

doomcore.reasoning: Every production choice in doomcore serves one purpose: to create the sensation of crushing, inescapable weight. The slow tempo (130-150) means each kick has time to fully develop its long, distorted tail — at 170 BPM gabber speed, there's no room for a 500ms kick decay. The extreme downtuning (-8 semitones or more) pushes the kick's fundamental into sub-bass territory (around 30-40Hz), where you feel it in your chest rather than hear it with your ears. The heavy distortion adds harmonics back into the upper registers so the kick remains audible on smaller systems while retaining that floor-shaking sub weight. The sparse drum patterns (often just kick on 1 and 3, or even just 1) create vast empty spaces that the atmospheric elements fill with dread — silence is weaponized. The minor keys and detuned pads trigger deep psychological unease. The lo-fi aesthetic (low sample rates, tape saturation, cheap reverb) removes the 'safety' of clean production — everything sounds damaged, deteriorating, hostile. The horror samples provide narrative anchoring, connecting the abstract sonic violence to concrete imagery of fear. The scooped mids create a 'hollow' frequency profile that the human ear interprets as unnatural and threatening — our brains expect mid-range content (voice frequencies) and its absence triggers discomfort. The long reverb tails suggest vast, empty spaces — cathedrals, caverns, abandoned industrial complexes — amplifying the isolation. The slow LFO modulation means nothing stays static but nothing moves fast enough to feel alive — it's the movement of geological processes, of decay, of entropy. This is music engineered to produce existential dread through sound design.
references: Frontal Sickness - The Mover
Astral Demons - The Mover
Doom Style - Disciples of Annihilation
Waves of Doom - The Mover
Cold Rush - Marc Acardipane
Nightflight (Non-Stop to Kaos) - The Mover
Transcontinental Doomcore Express - Passenger of Shit
Manu Le Malin - Various doomcore works
DJ Skinhead - Extreme Terror (slowed doomcore edits)
The Destroyer - various PCP releases
Doom's Day - Taciturne

gabber.name: Gabber / Early Hardcore
gabber.description: Gabber is the sound of Rotterdam losing its mind. Born in the early 1990s in the Netherlands — specifically in Rotterdam's underground rave scene as a violent, working-class answer to Amsterdam's polished house and trance — gabber (Dutch slang for 'mate' or 'buddy', borrowed from Yiddish) is electronic music at its most primal and confrontational. The genre crystallized around 1992-1993 at clubs like Parkzicht and through the legendary Thunderdome compilation series and events organized by ID&T. The formula is deceptively simple: four-on-the-floor kicks at 160-200+ BPM, but with the kick drum distorted into a massive, body-shaking weapon that simultaneously serves as the bassline. No subtlety. No restraint. Pure, mechanized aggression at speeds that blur the line between dancing and convulsing. The key figures are Paul Elstak (the 'Godfather of Gabber'), Rotterdam Terror Corps, Euromasters, The Prophet, DJ Dano, DJ Rob & MC Joe, Neophyte, and labels like Rotterdam Records, Mokum Records, and Mid-Town Records. The Thunderdome events (starting 1992) became the genre's cathedral — tens of thousands of gabbers in Australian tracksuits and Nike Air Max, shaved heads, doing the 'hakken' (a furious stomping dance where your feet barely leave the ground). The cultural dimension matters: this was explicitly anti-establishment, anti-pretension, working-class Dutch youth culture. Where Amsterdam had sophisticated club culture, Rotterdam had gabber — faster, harder, louder, dumber in the most liberating way possible. By the mid-90s gabber was a full cultural movement with its own fashion (Australian brand tracksuits, shaved heads, thunder logo), dance style, and massive events drawing 40,000+ people. The genre evolved — early gabber (1992-1994) was rawer and more chaotic; mainstream gabber (1994-1997) got more structured and commercially successful; then the scene imploded from oversaturation before being reborn through hardstyle in the 2000s. But that original 1992-1997 era remains one of the most extreme chapters in dance music history.
gabber.production: The gabber kick is the entire genre. Everything else is secondary. Here's how you build one: start with a sine wave at around 50-60Hz. Apply a fast pitch envelope — the kick starts at a higher pitch (200-400Hz) and sweeps down to the fundamental in about 30-50ms. This gives the attack its 'punch'. Now the crucial step: run the entire thing through HEAVY distortion. Not subtle saturation — we're talking waveshaper distortion, bit-crushing, or analog clipping pushed to absolute extremes. The distortion generates dense harmonics across the entire frequency spectrum, which is why the gabber kick sounds so full and massive — it's not just a bass drum, it's a wall of distorted harmonics from 40Hz all the way up to 8-10kHz. The classic hardware chain was a Roland TR-909 kick into a guitar distortion pedal (Boss DS-1 or similar), or sampling a kick and processing it in an Akai sampler with the gain cranked. Modern production uses waveshaper plugins, but the principle is identical. Kick decay is moderate (0.4-0.6) — shorter than doomcore because the tempo demands it. At 170 BPM each beat is ~350ms apart, so the kick tail needs to fit. The kick is typically tuned around -3 to -5 semitones from a standard 909 — lower than techno but higher than doomcore, keeping it punchy rather than subby. Beyond the kick: hi-hats are often just offbeat 8ths or straight 16ths, keeping time but not doing much else. Open hats on the offbeat are common. Snares/claps on beats 2 and 4, sometimes layered with a noise burst for extra aggression. The melodic content (when it exists) tends to be simple: hoover sounds (the classic Roland Alpha Juno 'What The' patch resampled and detuned — that tearing, screaming lead sound), stab chords, rave sirens, and vocal samples (often aggressive shouts, movie samples, or pitch-shifted vocals). The 'hoover' is the signature gabber synth — a detuned sawtooth sound with built-in chorus, usually played as a lead or stab, immediately recognizable from tracks like 'Mentasm' by Second Phase (Joey Beltram) which started the whole hoover trend. Arrangements are brutally direct: intro kicks, build with hats and FX, drop the hoover/melody, breakdown (sometimes with a cheesy vocal or sample), then back to the pummel. No long ambient intros — gabber gets to the point. Many tracks are 4-6 minutes of relentless pounding with minimal variation because the point is the physical experience of sustained BPM assault, not compositional sophistication.

kick.note: Four-on-the-floor at 160-200 BPM. The kick IS the track. Sine wave with fast pitch envelope (start ~300Hz, sweep to ~50Hz in 30-50ms), then CRUSHED through heavy distortion. The distortion harmonics fill the entire frequency spectrum — the kick is simultaneously the bass, the body, and half the midrange. Tune around -4 semitones from standard 909. Moderate decay — must fit the tempo.

snare.note: Beats 2 and 4. Snappy, aggressive — 909-style snare with added noise layer for bite. Not reverbed like techno snares — kept dry and punchy to cut through the distorted kick wall. Sometimes a clap is layered on top.

clap.note: Layered with snare on 2 and 4 for extra crack. Short, sharp. Some tracks use clap alone instead of snare — either works.

ch.note: Offbeat 8th notes — the classic pattern. Thin, metallic, high-passed aggressively so it sits above the kick's harmonic wall. Some tracks run straight 16ths but offbeat 8ths is more canonical gabber.

oh.note: Can replace or layer with closed hats on the offbeats. Slightly longer decay gives it a 'tsss' that creates forward momentum. Classic gabber uses open hats heavily.

ride.note: Optional. When used, provides a constant 8th-note pulse — a sheet of metallic texture above everything. More common in later/mainstream gabber.

bass.style: The distorted kick IS the bass. There is no separate bass synth in canonical gabber. The kick's distortion harmonics generate so much low-mid and sub content that adding a bass synth would create an unlistenable mess of clashing frequencies. This is the fundamental insight of gabber production: by distorting the kick to extremes, you create a single element that fills the role of kick, bass, and low-mid body simultaneously. Any 'bassline' movement comes from varying the kick's pitch, distortion amount, or pitch envelope per-step — essentially making the kick itself melodic.
bass.note: If you're adding a separate bass synth to gabber, you're doing it wrong. The kick tail owns everything below 200Hz. Melodic content lives above that — hoovers, stabs, leads.

modulation.lfoRate: N/A for main elements (gabber is largely static/relentless)
targets: kick pitch envelope amount (subtle per-section variation)
hoover detune amount
filter on breakdown pads
siren pitch (continuous sweep)
modulation.envelopes: The kick's pitch envelope is the most important modulation in the genre: fast attack, ~40ms decay, sweeping from 200-400Hz down to the fundamental. This creates the 'punch' that defines gabber kicks. Hoover sounds use slow detuning modulation for their characteristic 'tearing' quality. Otherwise, gabber is not a modulation-heavy genre — it's about relentless, unchanging force. The modulation IS the distortion — the waveshaper's transfer function creates dynamic harmonic content that responds to input level, giving the kick a living, breathing quality despite the static pattern.

mixing.loudness: Absolutely crushed. Gabber is one of the loudest genres in electronic music. The master limiter is working overtime. Target -4 to -2 LUFS — this should feel physically painful on a good system. The entire philosophy is maximum impact at all times. No dynamics, no breathing room, just wall-to-wall assault.
mixing.stereoWidth: Mostly mono. The kick is dead center and it IS most of the mix. Hi-hats can have slight stereo spread. Hoover leads benefit from stereo widening (chorus/detune creates natural width). But gabber is fundamentally a mono-center genre — it needs to hit like a freight train on any PA system, and stereo tricks weaken that center punch.
mixing.eqEmphasis: The kick dictates everything. Heavy 50-80Hz fundamental, dense 100-300Hz body from distortion harmonics, presence from the distortion's upper harmonics at 2-5kHz. High-pass everything else above 500Hz to stay out of the kick's way. The overall balance is mid-heavy and aggressive — not the scooped mids of doomcore but a full, in-your-face wall of distorted midrange. High end is present but not sparkling — the distortion adds grit, not air.
mixing.compression: The distortion IS the compression on the kick — clipping is the ultimate limiter. Bus compression is minimal because there's not much dynamic range to compress. Master chain: EQ (slight smile curve — boost sub at 50Hz, presence at 3-5kHz), hard clipper, brick-wall limiter at -0.1dB. Some producers use no master compression at all — just a limiter, because the individual element distortion has already destroyed all dynamics.

gabber.reasoning: Gabber's production choices all serve one function: maximum physical impact at maximum speed. The four-on-the-floor pattern at 160-200 BPM creates a pulse rate that approaches the human heartbeat at extreme exertion (160-180 BPM matches peak cardio), triggering a physiological fight-or-flight response — your body literally syncs to the tempo. The kick distortion is the genre's master stroke: by running a sine-based kick through extreme distortion, you generate harmonics across the entire spectrum from a single source. This means the kick, bass, and body of the track are phase-coherent (they're literally the same signal), eliminating the phase cancellation problems that plague multi-layered low-end approaches. It also means the kick cuts through any PA system — even on a phone speaker, the distortion harmonics are audible. The pitch envelope (high to low sweep in 30-50ms) exploits psychoacoustics: the initial high-frequency transient grabs attention while the settling low fundamental delivers the physical punch. The straight (no swing) quantization at these tempos is essential — at 180 BPM, any swing would create rhythmic confusion rather than groove. The brain needs rigid, predictable timing to lock onto a pulse this fast. The simplicity of arrangement (kick + hats + hoover) is functional, not lazy — at these speeds, complexity becomes noise. Each element must have its own clear frequency band because there's no time for the ear to separate overlapping elements between beats (~330ms at 180 BPM). The hoover sound works because its dense, detuned harmonic content creates a 'wall of sound' effect that matches the kick's density — smooth synth patches would sound weak against the distorted kick. The cultural connection matters too: the working-class Rotterdam audience wanted music that matched their energy — direct, confrontational, unpretentious. The production aesthetic mirrors this: no finesse, no subtlety, just overwhelming power. The hakken dance style (fast foot-stomping) only works with rigid 4/4 at high BPM — the dancers and the production co-evolved.
references: Poing - Rotterdam Termination Source (1992, the original gabber anthem — that bouncing bass sound over distorted kicks)
Amsterdam Waar LansenUll Hull - Euromasters (1992, Paul Elstak's parody/anthem of Rotterdam vs Amsterdam rivalry)
Mentasm - Second Phase / Joey Beltram (1991, invented the 'hoover' sound that became gabber's signature synth)
Don't Let Me Be Misunderstood - DJ Paul Elstak (1994, mainstream gabber crossover hit)
Neophyte - Number One (classic mid-90s gabber)
Rotterdam Terror Corps - You Wanna Be Hardcore (defines the attitude)
The Prophet - Big Boys Don't Cry (1995, euphoric gabber)
DJ Dano - Hardcore Will Never Die
Thunderdome compilations (I through X especially, 1993-1997 — THE canon of gabber)
The Stunned Guys - various Thunderdome anthems
DJ Rob & MC Joe - various early Mokum releases
Chosen Few - various releases on Mid-Town Records
Marc Acardipane / The Mover / Mescalinum United — bridging Frankfurt hardcore and gabber

uk_funky.name: UK Funky
uk_funky.description: UK Funky is London's mid-2000s answer to the question: what happens when you take UK garage's rhythmic DNA, strip the 2-step shuffle, and inject it with soca riddims, West African percussion, and tribal house energy? The result is one of the most rhythmically sophisticated dance music genres to emerge from the UK underground — a syncopated, percussion-heavy sound that sits at ~130 BPM and makes your body move in ways that four-on-the-floor never could. Born roughly 2006-2008 in London's pirate radio scene and club nights, UK Funky drew from a diaspora of influences: the soca and dancehall of London's Caribbean communities, the afrobeat and highlife rhythms of its West African populations, the broken beat scene's jazz-inflected syncopation, and the commercial appeal of funky house — all filtered through the bass-weight sensibility of UK garage and grime. The genre split into two main camps: a four-to-the-floor variant (closer to tribal/funky house, tracks like Crazy Cousinz's 'Do You Mind?' with Kyla) and the more revolutionary syncopated variant (the broken, off-grid kick patterns that defined the genre's unique identity — tracks by Roska, Funkystepz, Champion, and Lil Silva). The syncopated style is the real innovation — the kick drum deliberately avoids beats 1 and 3, instead landing on offbeats and 'e' and 'a' subdivisions, creating a lopsided, lurching groove that's impossible to stand still to. Key artists: Crazy Cousinz (the crossover hitmakers — 'Bongo Jam', 'Do You Mind?'), Roska (the producer's producer — his Rinse FM sets and Roska Kicks & Snares label defined the sound), Funkystepz (heavy, bass-driven funky), Champion (bridging funky and bass music), Marcus Nasty (DJ/tastemaker, Rinse FM), Lil Silva (who evolved from funky into broader bass music), iLL BLU (production duo), Donae'o (vocalist/producer who gave funky its pop crossover moments), and Tribal Magz. The scene lived on Rinse FM, Déjà Vu FM, and at nights like Heatwave and FunkyByNature. UK Funky had a brief but intense peak (~2008-2011) before its energy dispersed into UK bass music, future garage, and indirectly fed into the UK's later embrace of afrobeats and amapiano. But its rhythmic innovations permanently altered UK dance music's vocabulary — that syncopated kick pattern DNA lives on in everything from Disclosure's early work to modern UK club tracks.
uk_funky.production: UK Funky production is all about rhythm and space. The drum programming is the star — everything else serves it. Start with the kick pattern: in the syncopated style, the kick avoids the obvious downbeats. Instead of landing on steps 1, 5, 9, 13 (the four-on-the-floor positions), it lands on positions like 2, 7, 11 — creating a lurching, off-balance groove that pulls the listener forward. The kick sound itself is clean and punchy — not the distorted weapon of gabber or the boomy sub-kick of deep house, but a tight, controlled hit with moderate decay (0.3-0.4), tuned close to standard or slightly down (-1 to -2 semitones). Think of it as a hybrid between a house kick and a UK garage kick — present but not dominating, because the percussion needs room. And percussion is EVERYTHING in UK funky. Layered congas, bongos, shakers, cowbells, timbales, and woodblocks create polyrhythmic patterns directly influenced by West African and Afro-Caribbean music. These aren't just decoration — they're structural, providing the rhythmic complexity that makes the genre tick. Many producers sampled or synthesized tribal percussion, layering 3-4 percussion elements with different rhythmic cycles to create dense, interlocking grooves. The snare/clap is typically on beats 2 and 4 (steps 5 and 13), providing the only consistent anchor point while everything else moves around it. Hi-hats run in 16th-note patterns, often with velocity variation and subtle swing, creating a shimmering, busy top-end that contrasts with the sparse, syncopated kick. Bass is prominent but melodic — usually a sine or triangle sub-bass with a clean low-passed tone, following simple but effective patterns that lock with the kick. The bass often uses pitch slides and occasional octave jumps for movement. Vocal chops are a signature element: R&B vocals (often female) chopped into syllables, pitched up or down, and rhythmically rearranged into melodic hooks. Think of Crazy Cousinz's treatment of Kyla's vocals — the words become percussive melodic elements rather than lyrical content. Chord stabs are common — usually bright, funky house-style chords (minor 7ths, 9ths) with a short, plucky envelope, placed rhythmically to reinforce the syncopation. Steel drums, kalimba sounds, and other 'tropical' timbres appear frequently, reflecting the Caribbean influence. The overall production aesthetic is cleaner than grime but grittier than commercial funky house — there's a DIY energy from the bedroom/pirate radio origins, but the mixdowns are tight because the rhythmic complexity demands clarity.

kick.note: THE defining element. This is the syncopated variant — kick avoids the downbeats entirely. Landing on the 'e' of beat 1 (step 2/index 1), the 'a' of beat 2 (step 7/index 6), and the 'a' of beat 3 (step 11/index 10). This creates the trademark lurching, off-kilter groove. Clean, punchy, tight — NOT boomy. Moderate attack, short-medium decay. For the 4/4 variant, use steps [0,4,8,12] like house, but the syncopated version is what makes UK funky UK funky.

snare.note: Beats 2 and 4 — the rhythmic anchor. Tight, snappy, often a layered clap+snare. This is the one predictable element the dancer locks to while the kicks shift underneath. Sometimes rimshot or sidestick variants for a lighter feel.

clap.note: Layered with or replacing snare on 2 and 4. Short, tight, no reverb tail — UK funky keeps things dry and punchy to preserve rhythmic clarity.

ch.note: Constant 16th notes but with heavy velocity programming — accents on certain steps create a shimmering, humanized feel. The hats provide the metric grid that the syncopated kick plays against. Without steady hats, the kick pattern would lose its tension.

oh.note: Sporadic open hats add lift and air. Placed on offbeats to enhance the syncopated feel. Short decay — just a breath, not a wash.

conga_high.note: Essential. The high conga carries a dense, polyrhythmic pattern inspired by West African and Afro-Cuban percussion. This is what gives UK funky its 'tribal' character. Velocity varies — ghost notes between accents create a rolling, organic feel.

conga_low.note: Interlocks with the high conga to create a two-pitch call-and-response. The low conga often follows a pattern similar to the kick, reinforcing the syncopation in a higher register.

shaker.note: 8th-note shaker provides additional rhythmic texture above the hats. Tight, high-passed, barely there but adds to the polyrhythmic density.

cowbell.note: Pitched cowbell or agogo bell — another layer of the tribal percussion stack. Pattern often echoes the kick rhythm, creating sympathetic resonance between the low and high frequency syncopation.

bass.style: Clean, sub-heavy, melodic. The bassline in UK funky is prominent and melodic but never muddy — it needs to coexist with the syncopated kick without masking it. Bass patterns typically follow the kick loosely but add pitch movement: slides between notes, occasional octave drops on beat 1 for emphasis, and chromatic passing tones that give it a 'walking' quality. The bass is the harmonic foundation while the kick is the rhythmic foundation — they work in tandem but aren't always simultaneous.
bass.waveform: sine with slight triangle harmonic content
bass.note: Low-passed heavily — this is sub-bass territory, 40-80Hz fundamental with minimal harmonics. Clean and round. Some tracks use a Reese-style bass (detuned saws, low-passed) for a darker, more garage-influenced tone, but the classic UK funky bass is a clean sub that you feel in your chest. Pitch glide between notes (portamento ~30-50ms) is characteristic.

targets: hi-hat velocity/accent pattern
conga tuning (subtle pitch variation per hit)
bass filter cutoff (gentle movement)
vocal chop pitch/formant
chord stab filter
shaker panning
modulation.envelopes: Short, snappy envelopes on everything — this is a percussive genre. Kick: fast attack, 80-120ms decay. Congas: fast attack, 100-150ms decay, slight pitch envelope down for realism. Bass: fast attack, medium sustain, pitch envelope for slides (~30ms glide). Chord stabs: near-instant attack, 50-80ms decay for that plucky, rhythmic quality. Vocal chops: variable — some sustained, some chopped to 16th-note lengths. The overall modulation philosophy is restraint — UK funky lets the rhythm do the talking, not filter sweeps or wobbles.

mixing.loudness: Moderate loudness — UK funky needs dynamic range to let the polyrhythmic percussion breathe. The syncopated kick needs clear transient definition that over-limiting would destroy. Target -8 to -6 LUFS. This should feel punchy and clean, not crushed.
mixing.stereoWidth: Wide and detailed. The percussion stack is the key to stereo treatment: kick dead center, congas slightly left and right (high conga panned ~30% left, low conga ~20% right), shaker wide (~60% right), cowbell slightly off-center (~25% left). Hi-hats center or with subtle stereo variation per step. Bass mono, center. This creates a wide, immersive percussion field that makes you feel surrounded by rhythm — like standing in a carnival band. Vocal chops can be panned or use stereo delay for width.
mixing.eqEmphasis: Clean and balanced with emphasis on low-mid punch and high-frequency percussion detail. Sub-bass (40-80Hz) present and felt. Low-mid (100-250Hz) carefully controlled — the kick and bass must not clash here, so surgical EQ separation is essential. Mids (500Hz-2kHz) are where the congas, vocal chops, and chord stabs live — keep this clear and present. High end (8-16kHz) sparkles from the hats, shakers, and percussion transients. The overall tonality should feel 'bright and punchy' — not dark like dubstep or airy like deep house, but energetic and tropical.
mixing.compression: Light bus compression with fast attack to tame percussion transients without killing the groove. The kick needs to punch through — use sidechain compression on the bass from the kick (ratio 3:1, fast attack, 80ms release) to create space for the syncopated kick pattern. Individual percussion elements benefit from transient shaping rather than heavy compression — enhance the attack of congas and cowbell to keep them cutting through. Master chain: gentle EQ, light glue compression (1.5:1, medium attack), limiter. Don't over-compress — the magic of UK funky is in the dynamic interplay between percussion layers.

uk_funky.reasoning: Every production decision in UK funky serves its central innovation: the syncopated kick pattern and its relationship to layered polyrhythmic percussion. The tempo (~130 BPM) is carefully chosen — fast enough for energy and danceability, but slow enough to give the syncopated rhythms room to breathe and be felt. At 140+ BPM, the offbeat kick placements would blur together; at 120 BPM, the energy would sag. 130 is the sweet spot where you can feel every displaced kick hit and your body has time to react to the syncopation. The kick avoids downbeats because this creates rhythmic tension — your brain expects the kick on 1 and 3 (four-on-the-floor training from house music), and when it lands on the 'e' of 1 and the 'a' of 2 instead, your body involuntarily moves to resolve that tension. This is the same principle that makes soca and Afro-Caribbean rhythms so physically compelling — the displaced bass drum creates a sense of forward motion that a straight four-on-the-floor can't achieve. The layered percussion (congas, shakers, cowbell) serves multiple functions: it provides the polyrhythmic complexity borrowed from West African and Afro-Caribbean music traditions that were part of London's diaspora communities; it fills the rhythmic space that the sparse, syncopated kick leaves open; and it creates a dense temporal grid against which the kick's displacement becomes audible and meaningful. Without the steady hi-hats and percussion, the syncopated kick would just sound random — it's the contrast between the grid and the displacement that creates the groove. The clean, sub-heavy bass works because it can't compete with the kick for transient attention — a distorted or harmonically rich bass would mask the kick's rhythmic nuance. The sine sub lets you feel the low end while keeping the mid-range clear for percussion. The vocal chop technique comes directly from UK garage tradition (think Todd Edwards' micro-sampling) but applied with a more rhythmic, percussive intent — the vocals become another percussion layer rather than a melodic one. The relatively dry mix (minimal reverb on drums and percussion) preserves the rhythmic clarity that the genre demands — reverb would smear the precise timing relationships between the interlocking percussion parts. The Caribbean and African influences aren't just aesthetic choices — they reflect the actual communities that created this music in London, making UK funky one of the most genuinely multicultural genres in electronic music history.
references: Do You Mind? - Crazy Cousinz ft. Kyla (2008, the crossover anthem — 4/4 variant with pitched vocal chops and tribal percussion that brought funky to the mainstream)
Bongo Jam - Crazy Cousinz (2008, the name says it all — bongo-driven groove with funky house energy)
Blessed - Roska (2009, masterclass in syncopated kick programming — study the kick placement against the conga pattern)
Squark - Roska (the darker, bass-heavier side of funky)
In The Morning - Fuzzy Logik ft. Egypt (R&B vocal over tribal funky production — defines the genre's pop-facing side)
Heartbreak - Funkystepz (heavy, bass-driven funky with aggressive percussion)
Warrior - Champion (bridging funky and bass music, massive sub-bass with syncopated drums)
Night Skanker - Lil Silva (2010, the evolution point — funky's rhythmic innovation applied with future bass sensibility)
Seasons - Donae'o (vocal-led funky with pop crossover appeal)
Are You Gonna Bang Doe? - Funky Dee (2009, the viral anthem — stripped-back funky with MC vocal)
Heads Shoulders Kneez and Toez - KIG (2008, novelty crossover that introduced millions to funky's rhythm)
African Warrior - Marcus Nasty (tribal percussion showcase, deep and hypnotic)

footwork.name: Footwork / Juke
footwork.description: Footwork is Chicago's most radical rhythmic invention since house music — a genre built around 160 BPM that somehow feels both impossibly fast and deeply, hypnotically slow at the same time. Born in the late 1990s and early 2000s on Chicago's South and West sides, footwork evolved from juke (itself an evolution of ghetto house), but where juke was party music designed to make booties bounce at 150-160 BPM, footwork took that same tempo and warped it into something stranger, more abstract, more percussive, and inseparable from the competitive battle dance it soundtracks. The key distinction: juke is music you dance TO. Footwork is music that exists IN DIALOGUE with the dancer — the tracks are built with the footwork battle circle in mind, where dancers perform impossibly intricate, rapid-fire foot patterns that play off and against the music's rhythmic illusions. The founding figures are RP Boo (widely credited as footwork's inventor — his 1997 track 'Baby Come On' is considered the first true footwork track), DJ Rashad (the genre's greatest ambassador, whose 2013 album 'Double Cup' on Hyperdub brought footwork to global attention), DJ Spinn (Rashad's creative partner), Traxman (the prolific sample wizard), DJ Nate (whose 'Da Trak Genious' was an early international breakthrough), and the Teklife collective (founded by Rashad and Spinn, now the genre's primary institution). The scene lived in battle circles at events like the Battle Groundz, in park jams, and on YouTube where footwork dance videos went viral in the late 2000s. Juke's lineage runs through Chicago ghetto house producers like DJ Deeon, DJ Funk, DJ Slugo, and Waxmaster — nasty, booty-shaking 4/4 tracks at 150-160 BPM with hypersexual vocal samples. Juke kept the 4/4 kick but added syncopation and vocal chops. Footwork then REMOVED the 4/4 grid entirely, replacing the steady kick with scattered, unpredictable kick placements that create polyrhythmic conversations with the hi-hats, claps, and — most crucially — the vocal samples. The vocal sample is footwork's melodic instrument: a syllable, a word, a phrase grabbed from R&B, soul, pop, or rap, then looped, chopped, pitched, reversed, and stuttered into a rhythmic-melodic cell that repeats hypnotically. Think of DJ Rashad chopping an Aaliyah vocal into a 3-note fragment that loops every 6 steps against a 16-step grid — the resulting polyrhythm creates the genre's signature 'floating' feeling where the track seems to exist in multiple tempos simultaneously. Footwork went global around 2010-2013 through Planet Mu (Mike Paradinas released key compilations 'Bangs & Works Vol. 1 & 2'), Hyperdub, and the tireless touring of Teklife members. DJ Rashad's death in 2014 was a devastating blow, but the scene persists through DJ Spinn, DJ Taye, Jlin (whose 'Dark Energy' pushed footwork into avant-garde territory), and a global network of producers from Tokyo to London to São Paulo.
footwork.production: Footwork production is deceptively simple in its tools but profound in its rhythmic logic. The tempo is locked at ~160 BPM, but here's the critical insight: almost nothing in a footwork track actually moves at 160 BPM. The hi-hats run in triplet patterns (dividing each beat into 3 rather than 4), creating a 6/8 polyrhythmic feel OVER the 4/4 grid. The kicks are sparse — maybe 4-6 hits per bar rather than the 16 of gabber or the 4 of house. The vocal samples loop at intervals that don't align with the bar length. The result is multiple temporal layers moving at different speeds: the hi-hats suggest 120 BPM triplets, the vocal loop might imply 80 BPM half-time, the kicks stab unpredictably, and the nominal 160 BPM exists as a ghost grid that holds everything together without being directly articulated. This is footwork's genius — it's 160 BPM music that FEELS like 80 BPM with 160 BPM ornamentation. The production setup is historically minimal: many classic footwork tracks were made in Fruity Loops (FL Studio) with stock samples and ripped vocals. DJ Rashad, Traxman, and RP Boo all used FL Studio almost exclusively. The sample manipulation is the core skill — chopping vocal samples to exact lengths, pitching them up or down (often +3 to +7 semitones for that chipmunk quality, or -2 to -5 for a slowed, heavy feel), then placing them in patterns that create call-and-response with the drums. Reverb is used sparingly on vocals — a short room or plate to give them space without washing out the rhythmic precision. The bass is typically simple: a deep 808 sub-bass hit or sustained bass note, placed sparsely to reinforce certain kick hits. Some tracks have almost no bass at all — the rhythm IS the content. Synth pads or stabs occasionally appear but are never the focus. The overall mixdown is intentionally lo-fi in classic footwork — not polished or mastered to modern loudness standards, retaining the gritty, immediate quality of bedroom production. Newer footwork (Jlin, DJ Taye) has cleaner production but maintains the rhythmic philosophy.

kick.note: Sparse and unpredictable — this is NOT four-on-the-floor. Kicks land on beat 1 (step 0) for anchoring, then on off-grid positions (step 7 = the "a" of beat 2, step 10 = the "a" of beat 3). The pattern changes every 2-4 bars. Some sections drop the kick entirely, letting hats and vocal samples carry the groove. The kick itself is clean — a tight 808 or 909-style hit, not heavily processed. Short-medium decay because at 160 BPM there is no room for long tails. Sometimes a second kick pattern (pitched differently) interleaves for polyrhythmic effect.

snare.note: When present, on 2 and 4 — providing a half-time (80 BPM) backbone. But the snare is often ABSENT in pure footwork, replaced by claps, rimshots, or nothing. The half-time snare placement is what creates the "80 BPM inside 160 BPM" illusion that defines the genre.

clap.note: More rhythmically active than the snare — claps often fill syncopated positions, creating polyrhythmic interplay with the kicks. Tight, dry, sometimes layered in rapid-fire 32nd-note rolls for fills. The clap pattern shifts frequently throughout the track.

ch.note: The TRIPLET HI-HAT is footwork's rhythmic fingerprint. Rather than straight 16ths, the hats play patterns that imply triplet subdivisions — groups of 3 over the 4/4 grid. In a 16-step sequencer this is approximated by unevenly spaced hits that create a loping, rolling feel. Think of it as 12/8 time superimposed on 4/4. Velocity variation is crucial — accented hits on the triplet downbeats with ghost notes between. The pattern shown is an approximation; in practice, producers program these by feel in piano roll, not step sequencer, placing hats on exact triplet grid positions.

oh.note: Sparse open hats as accents, often on unexpected positions. Sometimes used in rapid-fire rolls (32nd notes) as transition fills between sections.

rimshot.note: Rimshots and wood-type sounds add another polyrhythmic layer. Common in classic RP Boo and Traxman productions. Placed to interlock with the clap pattern, creating a composite rhythm greater than either part alone.

bass.style: Minimal and sub-heavy. The bass in footwork is NOT a driving bassline — it's a punctuation mark. Typically a deep 808 sub-bass hit placed under key kick hits for weight, or a sustained sub note during vocal-heavy sections. Some tracks (especially juke-leaning ones) use a more active bass pattern, but pure footwork often has barely any bass at all — the genre is about RHYTHM, not frequency weight. When present, the 808 bass is clean, round, and pitched low (C1-F1), sometimes with a pitch bend on the attack for that classic trap/808 scoop.
bass.note: Pure sub-bass. No harmonics needed — the vocal samples and hats provide all the mid and high frequency content. If using a sustained 808, add slight distortion (0.1-0.2) to give it presence on smaller speakers. The bass is felt, not heard. Many classic footwork tracks literally have NO bass element.

modulation.lfoRate: N/A (footwork modulation is sample-based, not synth-based)
targets: vocal sample pitch (manual per-chop)
vocal sample start point
hi-hat velocity pattern
kick pattern variation per section
sample reverse/stutter
modulation.envelopes: Footwork is a sample-based genre — modulation happens through SAMPLE MANIPULATION rather than synthesizer parameters. The key "modulation" is: (1) vocal pitch shifting — each chop of a vocal sample may be pitched differently (+3, +5, +7 semitones for bright/manic feel, or -2 to -5 for dark/heavy), creating melodic movement through pitch variety rather than note sequencing; (2) sample start-point variation — the same vocal sample triggered from different start points each repetition, creating evolving textures from static material; (3) stutter/repeat effects — a vocal fragment repeated in rapid 32nd or 64th notes to create tension and rhythmic density before a drop; (4) reverse — samples played backwards as transitional elements. Synth envelopes are minimal because synths are minimal — if a pad appears, it's usually a simple sustained tone with slow attack, serving as harmonic bed under the rhythmic chaos.

mixing.loudness: Moderate to low by modern standards. Classic footwork was NOT loudness-optimized — tracks were made for battle circles and YouTube, not club systems. Target -10 to -7 LUFS. The lo-fi quality is part of the aesthetic. Newer footwork (Jlin, DJ Taye on Hyperdub) is more professionally mastered at -8 to -6 LUFS but still retains more dynamic range than most EDM.
mixing.stereoWidth: Mostly narrow/mono. Classic footwork production is nearly mono — made on headphones or cheap monitors in bedrooms. Vocal samples sit center or with slight panning variation between chops. Hats may have subtle stereo spread. The kick and any bass are dead center. The lack of stereo width is a feature, not a bug — it concentrates the rhythmic information into a focused, punchy center image that translates to any playback system. Newer productions use more stereo space for pads and atmospheric elements.
mixing.eqEmphasis: Mid-heavy and bright. The vocal samples occupy the primary frequency range (300Hz-5kHz). Hi-hats sparkle at 8-14kHz. The kick provides a sub-bass anchor at 40-80Hz but doesn't dominate. There's often a gap in the low-mids (100-300Hz) because there's no bassline filling that range — this gives footwork its characteristic "hollow" mid-section that makes the rhythms feel suspended in air rather than grounded. The brightness comes from the pitched-up vocal samples — at +5 semitones, a vocal originally centered at 300Hz now sits at ~400Hz with harmonics reaching into the high mids.
mixing.compression: Minimal. Classic footwork uses almost no bus compression or master processing — the tracks come out of FL Studio's mixer with basic level balancing and that's it. This is part of the raw, immediate quality. Individual samples may be normalized or gain-staged, but the overall dynamics are largely uncompressed. For modern footwork production, light master compression (2:1, medium attack) and a limiter are appropriate, but don't crush the transients — the hi-hat ghost notes and clap dynamics are essential to the groove feel. Sidechain compression is NOT used in footwork (unlike house/techno) — the sparse kick pattern doesn't need ducking space.

footwork.reasoning: Footwork's production logic is a masterclass in polyrhythmic illusion. The 160 BPM tempo is not arbitrary — it's the exact speed at which the human brain can be tricked into hearing BOTH a fast pulse (160) and a half-time groove (80) simultaneously. At 140 BPM, the half-time feel (70 BPM) would be too slow to groove; at 180, the half-time (90) would be too fast to feel languid. 160 creates a perfect 80 BPM half-time that sits in the golden zone of head-nod tempo — the speed of classic hip-hop. This is why footwork 'feels slow' despite being numerically faster than drum & bass. The triplet hi-hat pattern is the engine of this illusion. By dividing beats into 3 rather than 4, the hats create a 6/8 rhythmic layer that doesn't align with the 4/4 kick/snare grid. This polymetric friction is what gives footwork its hypnotic, 'floating' quality — your brain can't fully resolve the two rhythmic systems, so it oscillates between them, creating a trance-like state that mirrors the physical trance of competitive footwork dancing. The sparse kick placement serves two functions: (1) it removes the four-on-the-floor anchor that would 'solve' the polyrhythmic ambiguity and force a single tempo interpretation, and (2) it creates space for the DANCER to fill with their footwork patterns — the music and dance are co-composed, with gaps in the beat that the dancer's feet are expected to complete. This is fundamentally different from house or techno where the beat is a complete, self-sufficient entity. The vocal sample looping technique exploits a cognitive phenomenon called 'semantic satiation' — when a word or phrase is repeated enough times, it loses its linguistic meaning and becomes pure sound/rhythm. A chopped R&B vocal saying 'baby' 47 times stops being the word 'baby' and becomes a pitched percussion hit with melodic content. This allows footwork producers to create melodic phrases from non-melodic source material. The pitch-shifting of samples (+3 to +7 semitones) serves both aesthetic and practical purposes: aesthetically, it creates the manic, hyper-energetic quality associated with the genre; practically, it moves vocal content out of the frequency range where the kick and bass live, preventing masking. The lo-fi production quality isn't a limitation — it's philosophically aligned with the genre's origins in battle culture, where music was functional (made quickly, for immediate use in battles) rather than precious. A Traxman track might use 8 samples total and be arranged in 20 minutes, but the RHYTHMIC INTELLIGENCE of those 8 samples' placement represents years of deep listening to polyrhythmic music. The absence of bass in many tracks is the most radical choice: in a music landscape where bass weight equals power, footwork says rhythm alone is enough. The genre proves that temporal complexity can be more physically compelling than frequency weight.
references: Baby Come On - RP Boo (1997, widely considered the first footwork track — listen to how the kick pattern refuses to settle into a groove, constantly shifting)
Let U No - DJ Rashad (from Double Cup, 2013 — the most accessible footwork track ever made, lush vocal chops over rolling triplet hats)
I Don't Give A Fuck - DJ Rashad & DJ Spinn (battle circle energy — stripped back, raw, confrontational)
Feelin - DJ Rashad (pitched-up vocal sample as melodic instrument — study the loop length against the bar length)
Footworkin On Air - DJ Rashad (the groove is impossibly light despite the 160 BPM tempo)
Da Trak Genious - DJ Nate (2010 Planet Mu, one of the first footwork releases to reach international audiences)
Bangs & Works Vol. 1 - Various (2010 Planet Mu compilation, THE introduction to footwork for the world — every track is a masterclass)
Erotic Heat - Jlin (from Dark Energy, 2015 — footwork pushed into avant-garde territory, no samples, all synthesis)
Black Diamond - Jlin (polyrhythmic complexity taken to extremes — multiple interlocking patterns that shouldn't work but do)
Heavy Steppers - DJ Spinn (pure battle music — the gaps in the beat are for your feet)
Itz Crackin - Traxman (lo-fi sample mastery — an entire vibe from 4 chopped samples)
Off Da Hook - RP Boo (study how RP Boo's kick patterns create rhythmic tension through absence)
Acid - DJ Rashad & DJ Spinn (footwork meets acid house — 303 line at 160 BPM over triplet hats)
footwork.lineage:
### Genesis: From Ghetto House to Footwork
Juke/Footwork emerged from Chicago's ghetto house scene when pioneering DJs began playing records at 45 RPM instead of 33 RPM, creating an urgent, hyperkinetic sound. The genre crystallized between 1996-1998 through three foundational figures:
footwork.currentScene:
### Chicago: Post-Rashad Era
Following DJ Rashad's death in 2014, Chicago's scene has struggled with:
Youth migration to drill music: Local preference shifting away from footwork
Venue closures: Underground spaces becoming scarce
Economic pressures: Gentrification affecting core communities
Teklife continuity: DJ Spinn, DJ Taye maintaining legacy

dub_techno.name: Dub Techno
dub_techno.description: Dub techno is the sound of infinite space rendered through machines — the unlikely marriage of Jamaican dub's echo-chamber mysticism with Detroit techno's mechanical precision, forged in early-1990s Berlin. Where techno drives forward, dub techno hovers. Where dub is organic and loose, dub techno is gridded and hypnotic. The result is electronic music at its most meditative and immersive — tracks that feel like standing inside a reverb unit while a minimal techno loop dissolves around you. The genre was essentially invented by Moritz von Oswald and Mark Ernestus, who met through Ernestus's Hard Wax record store in Berlin and formed Basic Channel in 1993. Their early releases (initially under the pseudonym Maurizio) fused Detroit techno's skeletal 4/4 framework with the spacious, delay-saturated production techniques of Jamaican dub pioneers like King Tubby and Lee 'Scratch' Perry. The key insight: use the mixing desk and effects chain as instruments, not just processing tools. Every element — kick, hat, chord stab, bass — passes through cascading delays, reverbs, and filters that transform dry, minimal source material into vast, evolving soundscapes. The chord is the genre's signature element: a simple minor chord stab, usually from a Roland synth or similar, fed into a long delay chain with high feedback (80-95%) and a low-pass filter in the feedback loop. Each repetition loses high-frequency content, getting darker, muddier, more distant — like a sound disappearing into fog. The result is a shimmering, self-generating harmonic cloud that seems to breathe on its own. Basic Channel's label Chain Reaction became the genre's home, releasing early works from Monolake (Robert Henke), Vladislav Delay, and Substance. The influence spread globally: DeepChord (Rod Modell) in Detroit, Echocord label in Copenhagen, Yagya in Iceland (whose 'Rhythm of Snow' is a masterpiece of cold, Nordic dub techno), Shinichi Atobe in Japan, Fluxion in Greece, and Deadbeat in Canada. In the UK, Andy Stott and Claro Intelecto pushed dub techno into darker, bass-heavier territory through the Modern Love label. The genre has a unique relationship with texture and noise — hiss, crackle, tape saturation, and vinyl surface noise are embraced as musical elements, giving tracks a patina of age and warmth that contrasts with techno's clinical precision. Dub techno is often described as 'music for staring out of rain-streaked windows' — it's introspective, patient, and rewards deep listening. A single track might develop over 8-12 minutes with changes so subtle you don't notice them consciously, yet the emotional landscape shifts profoundly from beginning to end.
dub_techno.production: Dub techno production is fundamentally about DELAY AND FEEDBACK CHAINS. This is not a genre where you program complex patterns — you program simple, sparse patterns and then let effects processing generate the complexity. The signal flow is everything. Start with a dry chord stab: typically a minor chord (minor 7th or minor 9th for extra depth) from a warm analog or analog-modeled synth — Roland Juno-106, SH-101, or a polysynth with detuned oscillators. The stab itself is short, almost percussive: fast attack, very short decay (50-100ms), creating a rhythmic 'tick' rather than a sustained pad. Now the magic: route this dry stab into a delay unit with a LONG delay time (dotted 8th notes, quarter notes, or 3/16 at the track's tempo — the delay time choice determines the rhythmic character of the echoes), HIGH feedback (80-95% — the echoes should repeat many times, building on each other), and crucially, a LOW-PASS FILTER inside the feedback loop. This filter is set around 2-4kHz and sometimes modulated slowly. Each time the signal passes through the delay loop, it loses more high-frequency content, so the first echo is bright and present, the second is darker, the third murkier, and by the 8th or 10th repetition it's a deep, warm ghost of the original.
