That track — with its iconic bust-of-Helios album art rendered in pink and teal — became vaporwave's 'Smells Like Teen Spirit': the single artifact that defines the genre to outsiders. The BPM range is extreme because vaporwave encompasses two opposite approaches: classic vaporwave runs at 60-90 BPM (original sources slowed by 20-40%), while future funk — the genre's danceable offspring — runs at 110-130 BPM (original disco/city pop sources sped up or kept at tempo with added four-on-the-floor kicks). Future funk emerged around 2012-2013, pioneered by producers like Macross 82-99, Saint Pepsi (now Skylar Spence), and YUNG BAE. Where vaporwave is melancholic, dreamy, and deconstructive, future funk is joyful, danceable, and reconstructive — it takes the same source material (particularly Japanese city pop and 80s disco) but instead of slowing it down into a haze, it chops it into tight, punchy loops over compressed, sidechained house beats. Think of it as vaporwave's extroverted sibling: where vaporwave wanders the empty mall at 3am, future funk throws a party in the food court. Other key vaporwave subgenres: mallsoft (ambient vaporwave that simulates the reverberant acoustics of empty shopping spaces — think Disconscious's 'Hologram Plaza'), signalwave (extreme sample manipulation where the 'song' is essentially a collage of TV commercials, infomercials, and broadcast ephemera), and late night lo-fi (slowed R&B and jazz samples that heavily influenced the 'lo-fi hip hop beats to study to' phenomenon). Key artists beyond the founders: SAINT PEPSI / Skylar Spence (future funk's most polished producer — 'Hit Vibes' is a masterpiece), 猫 シ Corp (prolific conceptual vaporwave — 'Palm Mall' is essential mallsoft), t e l e p a t h テレパシー能力者 (ambient/late night vaporwave), 2814 (the collaborative project of telepath and HKE, whose '新しい日の誕生' is vaporwave's ambient masterwork), Blank Banshee (who fused vaporwave with trap beats creating 'vaportrap'), and Luxury Elite (whose warm, R&B-influenced productions bridge classic vaporwave and future funk).
vaporwave.production: Vaporwave production is fundamentally about SAMPLE MANIPULATION — this is not a genre of synthesis but of transformation. The core techniques trace directly to DJ Screw's chopped-and-screwed methodology and plunderphonics artists like The Avalanches and J Dilla, but applied with a specific aesthetic intent: to make the familiar strange, to transform commercial pop into ambient art. CLASSIC VAPORWAVE technique: (1) SOURCE SELECTION — find 80s/90s smooth jazz, R&B, city pop, corporate muzak, elevator music, new age, or lounge. The more 'corporate' and slick the source, the better — Diana Ross, Toto, Japanese city pop artists like Tatsuro Yamashita and Mariya Takeuchi, Kenny G, Sade, corporate training videos, Windows 95 startup sounds. (2) SLOW IT DOWN — pitch-shift the entire sample down 15-40% (not just tempo change — pitch AND tempo together, like literally playing a 45rpm record at 33rpm). This is the defining move. A pop vocal at -30% pitch becomes a ghostly, gender-ambiguous drone. A major-key melody becomes wistful and melancholic. The harmonics shift, the attack times lengthen, the transients soften — everything becomes dreamlike. In a DAW, this means timestretching with pitch-lock OFF (or using a dedicated vinyl speed plugin). (3) CHOP AND LOOP — isolate a 2-8 bar section of the slowed source and loop it. The eccojam technique (from Lopatin) loops a very short phrase (sometimes just 1-2 bars) with heavy echo/delay so the repetitions build on each other. More structured vaporwave arranges multiple loops from the same or different sources into a collage. (4) DEGRADE THE SIGNAL — add lo-fi processing: slight bitcrushing (12-bit for that 'early sampler' quality), tape saturation, vinyl crackle and hiss, low sample rate artifacts, VHS-style audio degradation (slight pitch wobble from wow and flutter, reduced bandwidth). Roll off the highs gently with a low-pass around 12-15kHz to simulate tape or vinyl playback. (5) DRENCH IN REVERB — long, washy reverb (3-8 second decay) with moderate-high wet mix (40-70%). This creates the sense of hearing music in a large, empty space — the abandoned mall, the hotel lobby at 4am, the liminal space between memory and dream. Some producers use chorus and flanger to add further dreaminess. (6) ADD ECHO — delay with moderate feedback (40-70%), often timed to the (now-slowed) tempo, creating cascading repetitions that blur the boundary between the original and its reflections. FUTURE FUNK technique differs significantly: (1) SOURCE — primarily Japanese city pop (Tatsuro Yamashita, Junko Ohashi, Anri, Meiko Nakahara), 80s disco, and boogie. The sources are brighter, more upbeat, more danceable than classic vaporwave's muzak. (2) TEMPO — kept at or near original speed (110-128 BPM), sometimes slightly sped up for energy. Pitch-shifting is minimal or upward (slightly brighter, more energetic). (3) CHOP TIGHTER — samples are chopped into shorter, more rhythmic fragments (1-4 bar loops with precise start/end points on beat boundaries). The chopping is more surgical than vaporwave's dreamy loops. (4) ADD A FOUR-ON-THE-FLOOR KICK — a punchy, compressed house kick layered under the sample, often with heavy sidechain compression so the entire sample 'pumps' with each kick hit. This is the single most important production difference from classic vaporwave. (5) SIDECHAIN EVERYTHING — aggressive sidechain compression from the kick, ratio 4:1 to 8:1, fast attack, 100-200ms release. The pumping effect is NOT subtle — it's a deliberate stylistic choice that gives future funk its bouncy, danceable energy. The entire sample ducks on every kick, creating that characteristic 'breathing' rhythm. (6) BRIGHT, COMPRESSED MIX — unlike vaporwave's lo-fi haze, future funk is mastered louder and brighter, closer to modern house music. The vintage source material provides warmth while the modern processing (compression, sidechain, EQ) provides punch and clarity.

kick.note: Classic vaporwave often has NO added drums — the original sample's drums (slowed down) provide the rhythm, or there are no drums at all (ambient/mallsoft variants). When drums are added, they're usually lo-fi drum machine hits (LinnDrum, 808, or deliberately cheap-sounding samples) with the transients softened. FUTURE FUNK uses a punchy, modern four-on-the-floor kick — clean, tight, 909-style or a compressed house kick with good sub-bass presence. The kick is the engine of future funk's danceability and the trigger for the sidechain compression that defines the sound. Moderate decay — long enough for weight but short enough to let the sidechain release create the pumping effect.

snare.note: In classic vaporwave: the snare from the original sample, slowed and degraded, is usually sufficient — no additional snare needed. When added, it's a soft, reverbed clap or snare with rolled-off transients to maintain the dreamy feel. In future funk: a snappy clap or snare on 2 and 4, brighter and more present than vaporwave, but still sitting behind the sample and kick in the mix. Some future funk tracks let the original sample's snare do the work.

clap.note: Layered with or replacing snare. In future funk, the clap is often the primary backbeat — a tight, compressed clap with a short reverb tail. In classic vaporwave, claps are rare as added elements.

ch.note: Classic vaporwave: usually absent or from the original sample. Future funk: programmed hi-hats at 8th or 16th notes, often with velocity variation. The hats provide the house-music grid that the chopped sample sits on. Kept fairly quiet — the sample itself provides most of the high-frequency content. Some future funk tracks use a shaker or tambourine loop instead.

oh.note: Offbeat open hats in future funk for added house energy. Absent in classic vaporwave. Short decay to avoid clashing with the sample's own cymbal content.

bass.style: Classic vaporwave: the bass IS the slowed-down sample's original bass — when you pitch a track down 30%, the bass drops into deep sub territory naturally, becoming a warm, woozy low-end presence. No additional bass synth is needed or desired — adding one would break the illusion that you're hearing a degraded recording. The slowed bass has a characteristic 'wobbly' quality from the pitch-shifting artifacts. Future funk: the original sample's bass is usually retained, supplemented by the kick drum's low end. Some future funk producers add a sub-bass layer (sine wave, 40-60Hz) to reinforce the kick, but this is subtle — the chopped sample provides the melodic bass content. The sidechain compression means the bass ducks on every kick, creating clean low-end separation.
bass.waveform: N/A (sample-based — the source material's bass, pitch-shifted)
bass.note: The bass in vaporwave is not synthesized — it's the original recording's bass, transformed through pitch-shifting. This is philosophically important: vaporwave doesn't CREATE sounds, it TRANSFORMS existing commercial music. The bass from a Diana Ross track, slowed 30%, becomes a deep, warm, slightly out-of-tune sub-bass that no synthesizer could replicate because it carries the harmonic complexity and imperfection of the original live performance.

targets: global pitch (slow wow/flutter — ±5-15 cents at 0.1-0.3 Hz simulates tape machine or turntable speed variation)
low-pass filter cutoff (gentle sweeps to simulate changing tape quality or 'underwater' moments)
reverb wet/dry mix (sections becoming more or less 'distant')
stereo width (slow panning or width modulation for disorientation)
bitcrusher depth (subtle degradation variation)
chorus rate/depth (enhancing the dreamlike, unstable pitch quality)
modulation.envelopes: Vaporwave is largely envelope-free because the source samples provide all the dynamic shaping. The 'envelope' is the original recording's dynamics, slowed down — transients become softer, decays become longer, everything smooths out naturally through the pitch-shifting process. In future funk, the sidechain compression creates a rhythmic amplitude envelope on the entire mix — fast attack (1-5ms), medium release (100-200ms) — that is the genre's primary dynamic modulation. The pumping IS the envelope. For added drums in either style: kick has a standard fast-attack envelope; snare/clap have slightly softened attacks (5-10ms) in classic vaporwave to maintain the lo-fi, dreamy aesthetic.

mixing.loudness: Classic vaporwave: quiet and dynamic. Target -12 to -8 LUFS. The lo-fi aesthetic demands breathing room — over-limiting would destroy the dreamy, submerged quality. The track should feel like it's playing from another room, through a wall, on a degraded tape. Future funk: louder and more compressed. Target -8 to -5 LUFS. The sidechain pumping and house-music energy demand a more assertive master. Still not as crushed as peak-time EDM, but punchy and present enough for dancefloor use.
mixing.stereoWidth: Classic vaporwave: the stereo image of the original sample is preserved but often narrowed or made mono-ish by the lo-fi processing. Heavy reverb adds width back, creating a contrast between a narrow, degraded source and a wide, atmospheric reverb field — like hearing a mono radio in a cathedral. Some tracks use deliberate stereo manipulation for disorientation. Future funk: wider and more defined. The sample provides the stereo content, the kick sits dead center, sidechain compression creates a unified stereo pumping. More conventionally 'produced' stereo image than classic vaporwave.
mixing.eqEmphasis: Classic vaporwave: dark and muffled. Low-pass at 12-14kHz to remove 'air' and brightness. The pitch-shifting naturally darkens the tone (the original's high harmonics are shifted down into the mid-range). Gentle high-shelf cut (-3 to -6dB above 8kHz). Warm low-mids emphasized by the pitch-shifting. The overall balance should feel like listening through gauze — present enough to hear details, but with a veil of warmth and softness that removes all clinical precision. Sub-bass is prominent but wobbly and unstable from the pitch artifacts. Future funk: brighter and punchier. The original sample's brightness is mostly preserved. The kick adds sub-bass weight. High-end is more present — the hat and cymbal content sparkles. Still warmer than modern house (the vintage source material ensures this) but significantly brighter than classic vaporwave.
mixing.compression: Classic vaporwave: minimal deliberate compression — the source recordings were already mastered, and the pitch-shifting/reverb processing adds its own dynamic smoothing. Some producers use light tape-emulation compression for warmth and glue. The dynamic range of the slowed sample naturally compresses because the transients are softened by the time-stretching. Future funk: heavy sidechain compression is THE defining production technique. The kick triggers sidechain compression on the entire sample bus: ratio 4:1 to 8:1, attack 1-5ms (fast enough to catch the transient), release 100-200ms (tuned to the BPM so the sample recovers just before the next kick). This creates the characteristic 'pumping' effect. Bus compression on the master (2:1 to 3:1, medium attack) glues the kick and sample together. The result should be bouncy and energetic, the entire track breathing with the kick pattern.

vaporwave.reasoning: Vaporwave's production choices are inseparable from its conceptual framework — every technique serves the genre's central meditation on nostalgia, consumer capitalism, and the uncanny. The SLOWING DOWN of commercial pop music is the foundational move, and it works on multiple levels simultaneously. Perceptually: pitch-shifting a major-key pop song down 20-40% transforms its emotional content — major keys become ambiguous, upbeat tempos become languid, confident vocals become ghostly and uncertain. The familiar becomes strange. Psychoacoustically: the lengthened attack times and softened transients from pitch-shifting trigger the brain's 'dreaming' associations — this is why slowed music feels 'dreamy', because it shares the temporal characteristics of auditory hallucinations in hypnagogic (falling asleep) states. The long reverb reinforces this — large-reverb environments feel 'unreal' because we rarely experience them in waking life except in unusual spaces (cathedrals, empty warehouses, abandoned malls). Culturally: the source material selection is deliberate — smooth jazz, elevator music, corporate muzak, and 80s pop represent the SOUND of late capitalism, the acoustic wallpaper of consumer spaces. By slowing it down and looping it, vaporwave forces the listener to actually HEAR music designed to be ignored — it turns background into foreground, commodity into art. The lo-fi degradation (tape hiss, bitcrushing, VHS artifacts) simulates the decay of physical media — the CD that skips, the VHS tape that's been copied too many times, the cassette warping in a hot car. This connects to vaporwave's obsession with FAILED FUTURES — the gleaming digital utopia promised by 80s/90s technology that never materialized. The degraded signal IS the broken promise. Future funk inverts vaporwave's melancholy by taking the same source material and making it danceable — but this isn't simple optimism. Future funk's heavy sidechain compression creates a MECHANICAL, FORCED danceability that layers a modern production technique over vintage source material, creating a temporal collision that's its own form of commentary. The sidechain pumping makes the 80s sample 'breathe' in the rhythm of 2010s house music — past and present literally compressed together. The tempo choice matters: future funk at 120-128 BPM places the chopped city pop samples into house music's temporal frame, recontextualizing Japanese pop from the 80s into contemporary club culture. The Japanese city pop fixation in both genres isn't arbitrary — these records represent a FOREIGN consumer culture's vision of Western luxury and sophistication, already one step removed from the 'real' thing, making them perfect material for vaporwave's hall-of-mirrors engagement with authenticity and simulation. Mariya Takeuchi's 'Plastic Love' IS vaporwave's thesis statement: a Japanese pop song about plastic, fake love, wrapped in gleaming production that itself became a viral internet artifact decades later — layers of mediation and nostalgia folding in on themselves. The use of Japanese text in track titles and artist names (even by non-Japanese producers) reflects both genuine appreciation and a broader engagement with the aesthetics of TRANSLATION and DISPLACEMENT — meaning that doesn't quite translate, beauty that doesn't quite resolve. The eccojam technique (short loop + heavy echo) creates a specific psychological effect: the repetition triggers semantic satiation (words lose meaning through repetition), while the delay creates a sense of temporal smearing — the present moment bleeding into its own past. This produces the distinctive vaporwave feeling of being 'stuck in time', looping through a half-remembered moment from a past that may never have existed.
references: リサフランク420 / 現代のコンピュー - Macintosh Plus / Vektroid (2011, 'Floral Shoppe' — THE vaporwave track. Diana Ross's 'It's Your Move' slowed to a narcotic crawl, chopped into stuttering loops, drenched in reverb. The album art — pink-and-teal bust of Helios with Japanese text — became the genre's visual icon. Study the exact amount of pitch-shift: roughly -25 to -30%, enough to make Ross's voice unrecognizable but retain its emotional core)

Chuck Person's Eccojams Vol. 1 - Daniel Lopatin (2010 — the founding document. Each track takes a short loop from an 80s pop song, slows it slightly, and layers it with cascading echo. 'A1' (sampling Chris de Burgh's 'Lady in Red') demonstrates how a 4-bar pop loop becomes an ambient meditation through repetition and processing. This IS the eccojam technique — minimal intervention, maximum transformation)

Far Side Virtual - James Ferraro (2011 — the conceptual extreme. Instead of slowing down pop, Ferraro CREATES muzak from scratch using the sounds of corporate technology: Skype ringtones, iPhone alerts, smooth jazz, new age. It's vaporwave as critique rather than collage. Pitchfork gave it Best New Music, which either validated or killed the genre depending on who you ask)

Hit Vibes - SAINT PEPSI / Skylar Spence (2013 — future funk's masterwork. Tight, polished, joyful chopping of city pop and disco samples over compressed house kicks. 'Cherry Pepsi' and 'Enjoy Yourself' are production masterclasses in how to make 30-year-old samples feel fresh. Study the sidechain compression: aggressive but musical, the whole mix pumping in perfect sync with the kick)

Plastic Love - Mariya Takeuchi (1984 — not vaporwave itself, but the SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT source record. This Japanese city pop track became a viral YouTube phenomenon in 2017, introducing millions to the sound that vaporwave had been sampling for years. The original production by Tatsuro Yamashita is already immaculate — warm, lush, disco-influenced pop with a melancholic edge. Every future funk producer has sampled this at least once)

A Million Miles Away - Macross 82-99 (2012 — early future funk that defined the template: chopped Japanese anime/city pop samples, four-on-the-floor kicks, heavy sidechain compression, and an unabashed love of 80s pop energy. More manic and maximalist than SAINT PEPSI's polished approach)

Palm Mall - 猫 シ Corp (2014 — the definitive mallsoft album. Ambient muzak slowed and reverbed to simulate the acoustic experience of wandering an empty shopping mall. Each track is named after a mall store. The reverb decay times are extreme — 8-12 seconds — creating cavernous, lonely spaces. This is vaporwave's ambient wing at its most evocative)

新しい日の誕生 (Birth of a New Day) - 2814 (2015 — vaporwave's ambient masterpiece. A collaboration between t e l e p a t h テレパシー能力者 and HKE that transcends genre into something approaching Eno-esque ambient. Slowed samples dissolve into pure texture over vast reverb spaces. Track titles in Japanese, cover art of nighttime Hong Kong skyline. This proved vaporwave could produce genuinely moving, serious art)

Blank Banshee 0 - Blank Banshee (2012 — the vaportrap landmark. Trap 808s and hi-hat rolls merged with vaporwave's sample manipulation and reverb-heavy production. 'Teen Pregnancy' fuses chopped pop vocals with hard-hitting trap drums. Opened the door for vaporwave's influence on mainstream hip-hop production)

I'll Try Living Like This - Death's Dynamic Shroud (2015 — maximalist, chaotic vaporwave that layers dozens of samples into overwhelming collages. The opposite of mallsoft's calm — this is sensory overload as artistic statement. Study the sample layering: 3-5 sources playing simultaneously, each at different pitches and speeds)
ＳＵＮＳＥＴ ＢＬＶＤ - Luxury Elite (warm, R&B-influenced vaporwave that smoothly bridges the gap between classic vaporwave and future funk — lightly slowed, tastefully reverbed, genuinely beautiful)

Sailorwave - Macross 82-99 (2013 — future funk's anime aesthetic crystallized. City pop samples over bouncy kicks with anime vocal interludes. The production is intentionally lo-fi compared to SAINT PEPSI, closer to the bedroom-producer origins of the genre)
vaporwave.lineage:
Parent Genres: Ambient, Downtempo, Chillwave
Emerged: ~2010-2011  
Geographic Origin: Internet-native (primarily US-based online communities)
Key Influences: 1980s new age music, elevator music, smooth jazz, corporate background music, Japanese city pop
vaporwave.currentScene:
Active Communities:
Bandcamp: Primary distribution platform with hundreds of active artists
Reddit: r/Vaporwave (100k+ members) for discovery and discussion  
Discord: Multiple servers for production collaboration
SoundCloud: Secondary platform for experimental variants

afro_house.name: Afro House
afro_house.description: Afro house is the sound of the African continent's spiritual and rhythmic heritage channeled through electronic dance music — a genre born in South Africa in the 1990s that fuses the four-on-the-floor pulse of house music with organic African percussion, call-and-response vocals in indigenous languages, and a deep spiritual warmth that distinguishes it from every other house subgenre. Where Chicago house was born in clubs and Detroit techno in bedrooms dreaming of Kraftwerk, afro house emerged from a post-apartheid South Africa where kwaito, mbaqanga (the 'township jive' of groups like Mahotella Queens and Makgona Tsohle Band), deep house imports, and tribal house all collided in the shebeens and clubs of Johannesburg, Durban, and Pretoria. The result was something that carries house music's dancefloor functionality but feels rooted in the earth rather than in machines — organic percussion layers (djembe, shakers, talking drums, udu, kalimba) sit alongside programmed drums, vocals chant and ululate rather than just sing, and the basslines are warm and round rather than aggressive or clinical. The genre's international breakthrough came through Black Coffee (Nkosinathi Innocent Maphumulo), whose refined, deep, melodic approach to afro house — subtle percussion layers, patient builds, soulful vocals, and an emphasis on FEELING over IMPACT — made him the most internationally recognized South African electronic artist, eventually winning a Grammy in 2022 for 'Subconsciously'. But Black Coffee represents just one pole of a wide spectrum. Culoe De Song brought a darker, more hypnotic approach — his 2009 debut 'A Giant Leap' on Innervisions (Dixon's label) was a landmark that showed international audiences the depth and sophistication of South African electronic production. Enoo Napa pushes the spiritual, percussive, deep end — his productions are meditative, layered, polyrhythmic tapestries where the percussion IS the melody. Da Capo bridges afro house and afro tech with pristine production and driving energy. Shimza brings festival-scale energy while maintaining authenticity. Black Motion (Thabo Mabogwane and Robert Sobukwe Mahlangu) create deeply spiritual, vocal-heavy productions rooted in Zulu and Ndebele musical traditions. Djeff Afrozila represents the Angolan/Portuguese-speaking African side of the genre. Boddhi Satva brings Central African (Congolese) rhythmic DNA. And the broader ecosystem includes Zakes Bantwini (whose 'Osama' became a global phenomenon), Caiiro, Kususa, Pablo Fierro, Hyenah, Floyd Lavine, and the Keinemusik collective (particularly &ME, Rampa, and Adam Port) who helped popularize afro house in European clubs through their Berlin-based sets. The genre sits in a family alongside several related but distinct South African electronic forms: kwaito (slower, hip-hop-influenced, 100-115 BPM, slang-heavy vocals), deep house (the warm, jazzy side — artists like Jazzuelle, Atjazz connections), afro tech (harder, more driving, more repetitive — Da Capo, Enoo Napa's harder material, sometimes overlapping with European tech-house), gqom (Durban's raw, dark, minimal cousin — see separate entry), and amapiano (the log-drum-driven, jazzy, piano-heavy genre that exploded globally from 2019, partially evolving from both afro house and gqom). What unites all afro house is a philosophical commitment: the percussion is not decoration, it's FUNDAMENTAL. The polyrhythmic layers aren't added on top of a house beat — they ARE the beat, with the four-on-the-floor kick serving as one voice in a larger rhythmic conversation inherited from centuries of African drumming tradition. This is what separates afro house from a European producer simply adding bongo loops to a tech house track — the rhythmic DNA is different at the structural level.
afro_house.production: Afro house production begins with PERCUSSION — not the kick, not the bass, the percussion. The layered, interlocking organic percussion patterns are the genre's soul, and everything else is built around them. Start with a foundation of 2-4 percussion voices: a djembe or conga providing the mid-frequency rhythmic anchor (tuned to resonate around 200-500Hz, with both open tones and muted slaps programmed for timbral variation), a shaker or cabasa providing high-frequency rhythmic continuity (8th notes or 16ths with velocity variation for a human feel), and often a talking drum, udu, or additional tonal percussion providing melodic-rhythmic content (pitched percussion that bridges the gap between rhythm and melody — this is a distinctly African musical concept that doesn't have a direct equivalent in Western production). These percussion parts should INTERLOCK polyrhythmically — each part has its own rhythmic cycle, and the magic happens where they overlap and diverge. A common approach: the djembe plays a pattern that groups in 3s (e.g., accents every 3 steps across a 16-step bar), the shaker plays straight 8ths providing the grid, and the talking drum plays a 5-note or 7-note cycle that creates constantly shifting accent patterns against the bar. This polyrhythmic layering is directly inherited from West African and Southern African drumming ensembles — it's not random complexity, it's structured conversation between voices. The kick drum sits UNDER this percussion conversation as a gentle, steady pulse — four-on-the-floor, but softer and rounder than in European house music. The afro house kick has moderate decay (0.35-0.45), is tuned close to standard or slightly down (-1 to -2 semitones), and is mixed at a level where it supports rather than dominates. Think of the kick as the heartbeat and the percussion as the breath — the kick provides the temporal framework, the percussion provides the life. Claps or snares on beats 2 and 4 are optional and often understated — in many afro house tracks, the backbeat comes from the percussion layers themselves (a louder djembe hit, a shaker accent) rather than a distinct snare/clap. When a clap is used, it's warm, slightly reverbed, and sits back in the mix. The bass is warm, round, and melodic — NOT the aggressive, distorted bass of techno or the minimal sine sub of deep house. Afro house bass typically uses a filtered sawtooth or triangle wave with moderate cutoff (0.3-0.45), creating a tone with enough harmonic content to have character and warmth but not so much that it competes with the percussion's mid-range. The bass pattern is MELODIC — it moves, it sings, it follows chord changes with stepwise motion and occasional octave drops. Think of the bass as another voice in the ensemble, not just a frequency filler. Common bass movement: root-fifth patterns, pentatonic phrases (the pentatonic scale is fundamental to much African melodic tradition and gives afro house bass its distinctive 'warm' quality), and call-and-response with the vocal or melodic elements. The vocal is sacred in afro house. Vocals typically feature African languages — Zulu, Xhosa, Ndebele, Yoruba, Swahili, Lingala — sung in styles that range from traditional chanting and call-and-response to soulful, gospel-influenced singing. The vocal is rarely processed into unrecognizability; it retains its human character, often with just reverb and subtle delay to give it space. Vocal chops (short phrases looped as rhythmic-melodic elements) are common — a single Zulu phrase repeated and layered becomes a hypnotic mantra that drives the track. Male and female vocals often appear together in call-and-response structures. Ululation (the high-pitched trilling vocal sound from Southern and East African traditions) appears as both a sample element and a textural flourish. Melodic instruments: piano (particularly Rhodes and warm electric piano voices), kalimba (the thumb piano — its bright, metallic, slightly detuned tone is an afro house signature), marimba, flute, and saxophone all appear. These melodic elements typically play pentatonic or natural minor phrases with a degree of improvisation — not locked to a rigid sequence but having slight timing variations that give them a 'played' rather than 'programmed' feel. Black Coffee's productions are masterclasses in restraint with melodic elements — a single Rhodes chord change, a subtle bass movement, a vocal phrase, and vast space between them. The arrangement philosophy is PATIENCE. Afro house tracks build slowly — 7-10 minutes is standard, with the first 2-3 minutes devoted to gradually introducing percussion layers one by one. Elements enter and exit in 16 or 32-bar cycles. The build is not toward a 'drop' (this is not EDM) but toward a state of COMMUNAL TRANCE — the point where all the polyrhythmic layers lock in, the vocal reaches its most intense expression, and the dancefloor becomes a unified body. The breakdown strips back to percussion and vocals — a moment of spiritual intensity before the full groove returns.

kick.note: Four-on-the-floor but SOFT and warm. This is not a driving techno kick — it's a supportive pulse beneath the percussion. Round, with a gentle low-end swell rather than a sharp transient. Think 909 with the tone rolled off and the attack slightly softened. Decay at 0.4 gives it enough body to feel present without competing with the bass. Mixed 3-4dB lower than in European house — the kick is ONE element in the rhythm, not THE element. Some afro house tracks (particularly the deeper, Black Coffee-influenced style) reduce the kick's presence even further, almost burying it beneath the percussion.

clap.note: Warm, slightly reverbed clap on beats 2 and 4. Not the snappy, dry clap of tech house — this has BODY, with a short reverb tail (200-400ms, plate or room) that gives it space and depth. Often layered with a subtle finger-snap or soft snare for texture. Some tracks omit the clap entirely, letting the percussion handle all the rhythmic accenting. When present, it sits BEHIND the percussion in level — the backbeat is suggested, not hammered.

ch.note: Sparse offbeat 8ths rather than continuous 16ths — afro house keeps the hat space open for the organic shaker and percussion to fill the high-frequency rhythmic role. The hat is quiet, almost subliminal, providing just a whisper of metallic time-keeping. Some tracks omit programmed hats entirely, using only organic shakers and tambourine for high-frequency rhythm. When present, the hat should be high-passed and quiet — a shimmering presence, not a driving force.

oh.note: Very sparse open hats — just one or two per bar as gentle accents. The open hat's longer decay provides a brief lift that contrasts with the tight closed hats and dry percussion. Reverbed slightly for space.

djembe.note: THE soul of afro house percussion. The djembe (or conga, depending on regional influence) plays a syncopated pattern that interlocks with the kick but has its own rhythmic logic. The pattern shown creates a 3+3+2 feel in places (hits on 0, 3, 7 create tresillo fragments) while also landing with the kick on downbeats (0, 4, 8, 12) for reinforcement. CRUCIAL: alternate between open tones (resonant, pitched) and muted slaps (short, dry, higher-pitched) for timbral variation — real djembe playing uses at least 3 distinct articulations (bass, tone, slap), and programming should reflect this. Velocity variation is essential — accent patterns within the cycle create musical phrasing that makes the percussion feel alive. The djembe should be EQ'd to sit in the 200-800Hz range with its attack transients reaching up to 2-4kHz.

shaker.note: 8th-note shaker providing high-frequency rhythmic continuity. This replaces the hi-hat's traditional role in house music — the organic shaker sound (cabasa, caxixi, or shekere) gives afro house its warm, natural high-end texture instead of metallic hats. Heavy velocity programming: louder on downbeats, quieter ghost notes between, with occasional 16th-note fills that create human-feel variations. The shaker should sound like a person playing it, not a machine triggering it. High-passed at 3-4kHz to keep it out of the percussion's mid-range.

talking_drum.note: Pitched percussion providing melodic-rhythmic content — a talking drum, udu, or tuned woodblock that bridges rhythm and melody. The pattern is deliberately offset from both the kick and the djembe, creating a third rhythmic layer that interlocks with but doesn't duplicate the other two. Each hit can be at a slightly different pitch (±2-4 semitones) to create a melodic contour within the rhythmic pattern. This is the element that gives afro house its distinctly AFRICAN rhythmic character — the concept of rhythm as inherently melodic is central to many African musical traditions.

rimshot.note: Additional percussion accent — a rimclick, side-stick, or woodblock providing yet another polyrhythmic voice. Placed at positions that fill gaps in the composite rhythm, contributing to the sensation of near-continuous rhythmic activity where no single voice is ever too busy but the ensemble is always moving. Optional — some afro house tracks keep the percussion stack to 2-3 voices for a cleaner, deeper feel.

bass.style: Warm, melodic, and round. The afro house bassline is not a static root-note pulse — it SINGS. Typical patterns use pentatonic phrases (root, minor 3rd, 4th, 5th, minor 7th) that move stepwise with occasional octave drops for emphasis. The bass locks with the kick but also responds to the vocal melody — in the best afro house productions, the bass and vocal are in conversation, with the bass answering vocal phrases or mirroring their melodic contour. The tone is warm, filtered, and organic-sounding — closer to an upright bass in character than a synth bass. Portamento (30-50ms glide) between notes adds fluidity. The bass occupies the 40-120Hz range as a fundamental with gentle harmonics extending to 300Hz for warmth. Some productions use a Moog-style bass (filtered sawtooth) for a slightly more present, harmonically rich tone; others use a cleaner sine/triangle for a deeper, more minimal feel. The key is WARMTH — the bass should feel like it's embracing you, not punching you.
bass.note: The cutoff is higher than dub techno or minimal but lower than tech house — afro house bass has presence and character but never brightness or aggression. The slight resonance (0.15) adds a gentle vowel quality to each note, giving the bass an almost vocal warmth. Envelope modulation at 0.2 provides a subtle 'bloom' on each note's attack — the filter opens slightly with each trigger, creating a gentle percussive quality that helps the bass cut through the dense percussion layers without adding harshness. Portamento between notes is essential — the glide gives the bass a human, singing quality that connects it to the vocal tradition in African music.

modulation.lfoRate: 0.05-1 Hz (slow, organic movement — breathing, not pumping)
targets: percussion velocity patterns (subtle humanization — each hit varies ±5-15% in velocity)
shaker timing (micro-timing offsets ±5-10ms for human feel)
bass filter cutoff (gentle opening and closing over 8-16 bars, creating harmonic breathing)
vocal reverb send (gradually increasing depth as the track builds)
djembe tuning (subtle ±5-10 cent pitch variation per hit for organic quality)
pad filter cutoff (slow sweeps over 16-32 bars for evolving atmosphere)
kalimba/piano volume (gentle swells that follow the arrangement arc)
modulation.envelopes: Afro house modulation serves one goal: making everything feel ALIVE and ORGANIC. The percussion envelopes are fast but varied: djembe open tones have ~5ms attack and 100-200ms decay; slaps have ~2ms attack and 40-60ms decay; the shaker has near-instant attack and 20-40ms decay. The bass envelope is gentle: 5-10ms attack (slightly softened, not the instant attack of techno bass), moderate sustain, with a filter envelope that opens ~200Hz over 50-80ms on each note. Pads (when present) use slow attacks (200ms-1s) and long releases (1-3s), creating a bed of warmth that never calls attention to itself. The overall philosophy: NOTHING should sound programmed. Every parameter should have subtle, continuous variation that mimics the natural inconsistency of live performance. The swing at 58% is crucial — it pushes the offbeat 16ths just late enough to create a loping, organic groove without becoming sloppy. This swing value is higher than tech house (30%) but lower than Chicago house (65%), sitting in a zone that feels simultaneously driving and relaxed.

mixing.loudness: Moderate — afro house is not a loudness-war genre. Target -9 to -7 LUFS. The dynamic range must be preserved for the subtle velocity variations in the percussion layers to be audible — if you crush the master, the ghost notes in the djembe pattern disappear and the percussion loses its LIFE. The track should feel warm and enveloping, not loud and aggressive. The buildup from sparse intro to full groove should represent a genuine dynamic arc, not just a frequency arc. Black Coffee's masters are typically around -8 LUFS — loud enough for the club but with enough headroom for the details to breathe.
mixing.stereoWidth: Wide and immersive — the percussion layers create a natural stereo field. Kick and bass mono center (non-negotiable — the low end must be phase-coherent). Djembe slightly off-center (~15-20% left or right). Shaker panned opposite (~20-30% right or left). Talking drum/additional percussion placed to create a balanced stereo percussion image — imagine the listener standing in the center of a drum circle, with each percussionist in a slightly different position. The vocal sits center or slightly off-center. Pads and atmospheric elements spread wide. Reverb returns are full stereo. The result should feel SPATIAL — like being in a room where real musicians are playing around you, not like a produced electronic track. Afro house's stereo image is one of its most distinctive qualities compared to the relatively narrow mixes of gqom or the clinical positioning of tech house.
mixing.eqEmphasis: Warm, full, and balanced with emphasis on the mid-range percussion detail. Sub-bass (40-80Hz) present and warm from the bass and kick — not overwhelming, but you should FEEL it. Low-mids (100-300Hz) are critical — this is where the djembe's body lives, where the bass's warmth resides. Don't scoop the low-mids like in techno; afro house needs this warmth. Mids (500Hz-3kHz) are where the percussion character lives — the djembe's tone and slap, the talking drum's pitch content, the vocal's body. Keep this range CLEAR and present. Presence (3-6kHz) from the shaker, percussion transients, and vocal clarity. High end (8-16kHz) gentle and natural — the organic percussion provides all the sparkle needed without any high-shelf boost. The overall tonality should feel like WOOD and SKIN — warm, resonant, organic — rather than the metal-and-plastic feel of European electronic music.
mixing.compression: Light and transparent. The WORST thing you can do to afro house is over-compress it — the genre's magic is in the dynamic interplay between percussion voices, and compression destroys that interplay by leveling the velocity variations that create the groove. Light bus glue compression (1.5:1, slow attack 30ms to let transients through, auto-release) is sufficient. Sidechain compression from the kick is subtle — 2:1 ratio, fast attack, 80-100ms release, just enough to create a gentle breathing without audible pumping. The pumping sidechain of tech house or future funk would destroy afro house's organic feel. Individual percussion elements benefit from transient shaping (enhance attack slightly on the djembe for clarity, reduce attack on the shaker for smoothness) rather than heavy compression. The bass may need gentle compression (3:1, medium attack) to keep it even across its melodic movement. Master chain: gentle EQ (slight warmth boost at 200Hz, slight presence at 3kHz), transparent glue compressor, limiter with 1-2dB gain reduction maximum. The master should sound like it was barely touched.

afro_house.reasoning: Every production choice in afro house serves a single overarching principle: the music must feel ALIVE, COMMUNAL, and CONNECTED TO THE EARTH. This is not abstract philosophy — it's a direct production methodology inherited from African musical traditions where music is participatory, organic, and spiritually functional. The polyrhythmic percussion layers are the genre's deepest structural element. In Western electronic music, rhythm is typically hierarchical: the kick is the primary pulse, the snare provides the backbeat, and hi-hats fill in. In afro house (following African drumming tradition), rhythm is CONVERSATIONAL: each percussion voice has equal structural importance, each follows its own rhythmic cycle, and the musical event is the COMPOSITE pattern that emerges from their interaction. A djembe pattern in 3s against a shaker in 2s against a talking drum in 5s creates a composite rhythm that cycles at the LCM (least common multiple) of those values — meaning the exact same combination of hits might not repeat for 30 or more steps, creating an endlessly evolving rhythmic texture from simple individual parts. This is why afro house percussion feels 'alive' where a simple conga loop feels 'added on' — the interlocking structure creates genuine polyrhythmic complexity rather than decorative layering. The tempo range (115-128 BPM) sits slightly slower than European house (125-130) and faster than kwaito (100-115), placing afro house in a groove zone that allows the polyrhythmic detail to be felt. At 130+ BPM, the interlocking percussion patterns would blur together; below 115, the energy for sustained dancing drops. The 118-124 sweet spot gives each percussion hit enough temporal space to be perceived individually while maintaining enough momentum for extended dancefloor movement. The swing value (58%) is critical and higher than most European house music because it aligns with the natural timing feel of African percussion traditions — the 'beat' in traditional African music is not metronomically rigid but has a characteristic lope or sway that Western music theory struggles to notate. This swing doesn't come from a percentage setting on a drum machine — it comes from internalizing the feel of how real djembe players place their notes relative to a pulse. 58% is the closest approximation in a quantized grid. The warm, melodic bass reflects the genre's roots in South African deep house and mbaqanga — both traditions feature prominent, singing basslines that carry melodic weight. The pentatonic scale preference connects to the melodic system of many African musical traditions (the same pentatonic vocabulary that gives blues its character, via the forced cultural exchange of slavery and colonization — a connection that gives afro house's harmonic language a deep historical resonance). The vocal tradition — singing in African languages, chanting, call-and-response — is not decorative 'world music' flavoring but a continuation of musical practices where the voice is the primary instrument and lyrics carry spiritual, communal, and historical meaning. When a Zulu vocal chants over an afro house beat, it's connecting a 21st-century dancefloor to centuries of musical practice — this is what gives the genre its emotional weight and distinguishes it from a European producer sampling 'ethnic vocals'. The soft kick and restrained mixing philosophy (lower LUFS, less compression, wider dynamics) serve the communal function: afro house is designed for EXTENDED DANCING in social contexts — club nights, outdoor events, festivals — where the music runs for hours. Aggressive, loud, compressed music causes ear fatigue and physical exhaustion over time; afro house's moderate loudness and dynamic range allow the body and ears to sustain engagement over 6-8 hour sessions. The music works WITH the body rather than ASSAULTING it. Black Coffee's mastery of this principle — his sets famously build over hours, never peaking too early, always maintaining space — demonstrates how afro house's production values serve a specific dancefloor function. The genre's branching into afro tech (harder, more repetitive, more European-influenced — Da Capo, Enoo Napa's driving productions) reflects the natural tension between authenticity and global appeal: afro tech takes afro house's percussion DNA and applies it to tech house's more driving, DJ-tool-oriented framework, making it more functional for European DJs while retaining the organic percussion character. This is not cultural dilution but cultural conversation — the same conversation that has always happened when African music meets Western technology, from jazz to hip-hop to house music itself.
references: Superman - Bucie ft. Black Coffee (2009, the early anthem — soulful vocal over deep, patient afro house production. Black Coffee's production here is a masterclass in restraint: warm bass, subtle percussion, and space for the vocal to shine. Drake later sampled this for 'Get It Together' on More Life, bringing afro house DNA to global pop)

Wish You Were Here - Black Coffee ft. Msaki (2021, from 'Subconsciously' — the Grammy-winning album. Delicate, emotional, spacious production. Study the percussion layering: barely there, but every element is precisely placed. The vocal floats over a bed of warm bass and gentle rhythmic conversation)

A Giant Leap - Culoe De Song (2009, Innervisions — the album that introduced afro house to the European underground via Dixon's label. Darker, more hypnotic, more polyrhythmically complex than Black Coffee's approach. 'Elevation' builds a trance-inducing percussion stack over 8 minutes. 'Webaba' is spiritual, percussive, transcendent)

Tinnitus - Culoe De Song (haunting, atmospheric afro house — a shimmering pad, a distant vocal, and interlocking percussion that pulls you into a meditative state. Demonstrates the genre's more introspective, spiritual side)

Osama - Zakes Bantwini ft. Kasango (2021 — the global phenomenon. That bassline, that vocal, that percussion — built for sunrise moments at outdoor festivals. Became an anthem across continents, proving afro house's universal appeal while remaining rooted in South African musical identity)

Da Capo - Secret (Enoo Napa Remix) (the harder, more driving afro tech side — Enoo Napa strips the original to its percussive essentials and rebuilds it with a relentless, hypnotic polyrhythmic groove. Study the percussion programming: 4-5 voices interlocking with mathematical precision that somehow feels organic)

Joy Joy - Black Motion ft. Brenden Praise (spiritual afro house at its most powerful — gospel-influenced vocals over deep, percussive production. The call-and-response vocal structure directly mirrors traditional South African musical forms)

Imali - Black Coffee ft. Maxine Ashley & Sun-El Musician (2021 — restrained, emotional, patient. The percussion enters gradually over the first 3 minutes, each layer adding to the rhythmic conversation. The bass is warm and melodic, following the vocal's contour)

10 Missed Calls - Black Coffee ft. Pharrell Williams & Jozzy (2021 — afro house meeting global pop without compromising its essence. The production retains its organic percussion and warm bass while the vocal brings pop accessibility)
Ibanathi - Shimza (festival-energy afro house with driving percussion and a massive build — shows the genre's capacity for peak-time impact while maintaining polyrhythmic integrity)

Sondela - Djeff ft. Brenden Praise (Afro-Lusophone afro house — Angolan/Portuguese-speaking African perspective, with slightly different percussion choices reflecting Central/West African rhythmic traditions rather than Southern African ones. The vocal is devotional, the production warm and lush)
Mama - Black Coffee ft. Usher (crossover afro house — R&B vocals over deep, percussive production that proves the genre can accommodate diverse vocal styles without losing its identity)
My Name Is - DJ Zinhle ft. Busiswa (energetic, vocal-driven afro house with heavy percussion and a commanding vocal performance — represents the genre's more upfront, club-ready side)

psytrance.name: Psytrance / Darkpsy
psytrance.description: Psytrance (psychedelic trance) is electronic music built on an unbroken, hypnotic engine of kick and rolling bassline — the bassline IS the track, pulsing in relentless 16th notes at 142-155 BPM, carrying the harmonic movement, the energy arc, and the psychedelic tension all at once. Originating in the early 1990s from the Goa trance scene in India (Goa Gil, Raja Ram, Astral Projection) and then spreading to Israel (Infected Mushroom, Astrix, Vini Vici), South Africa, Brazil, Japan, and Scandinavia, psytrance is the most ritualistic form of electronic dance music — designed for outdoor festivals and multi-day gatherings where the music runs continuously from sunset to sunrise and beyond. The genre branched into subgenres along a spectrum: 'Full On' (148-150 BPM, melodic, euphoric, big leads — Astrix, Vini Vici, Ace Ventura), 'Progressive Psytrance' (138-142 BPM, deeper, groovier, minimal — Neelix, Querox, Phaxe), 'Twilight' (145-148 BPM, darker than full-on but not as extreme as darkpsy — Burn In Noise, Jahbo), 'Forest' (148-155 BPM, organic textures, tribal, mushroom-ceremony energy — Derango, Parvati Records), and 'Darkpsy' (148-160+ BPM, atonal, chaotic, maximum weirdness — Parasense, Parvati Records, Kindzadza, Psykovsky). Darkpsy deserves special attention: it abandons melody entirely in favor of atonal, alien sound design. Where full-on psytrance has uplifting leads and harmonic progressions, darkpsy is built on squelches, screams, glitches, and textures that sound like machinery breaking down in a haunted forest. The bassline in darkpsy is often more aggressive, sometimes using FM synthesis or extreme resonance to create sounds that are physically unsettling. Labels like Parvati Records (Denmark/India), Sangoma Records, and Sonic Loom define the darkpsy aesthetic. The culture is inseparable from the music — psytrance is festival music, outdoor music, designed for UV-reactive decorations, projection mapping, and altered states of consciousness.
psytrance.production: The kick-bass relationship is THE defining production challenge in psytrance and what separates amateur from professional productions. The kick and bass must be phase-aligned and sidechain-locked into a single unified pumping entity. The kick is short and punchy (decay 0.15-0.25) — much shorter than techno kicks — because it needs to leave space for the bass notes that follow immediately. The standard technique: the kick occupies roughly the first 30-50ms of each 16th note, then the bass fills the remaining space. This creates the characteristic 'duh-duh-duh-duh' rolling pattern that IS psytrance. Sidechain compression is applied to the bass from the kick, but the release must be extremely fast (5-15ms) — you're not creating the pumping effect of EDM sidechaining, you're creating a tight pocket where the kick punches through and the bass immediately swells back. Many producers use volume automation or LFO tool instead of sidechain compression for more precise control. The bass itself is typically a monophonic synth line using saw, square, or FM-synthesized waveforms run through a resonant low-pass filter with heavy envelope modulation — the filter cutoff sweeps create the melodic movement. The 'acid' influence is direct: TB-303-style filter sweeps are foundational, but pushed into more extreme territory with higher resonance and faster modulation. In darkpsy, the bass often uses FM synthesis (2-3 operators) with the modulation index being automated wildly, creating those characteristic alien squelch sounds. Triplet patterns (16th-note triplets) are used extensively in bass programming to create tension and rhythmic surprise against the straight 4/4 kick — this is what gives psytrance its 'rolling' feel. The arrangement follows a strict energy arc: intro (atmospheric, kick enters), buildup (bass enters, layers add), climax section, breakdown (the bass drops out — this is the moment of tension), and then the drop back in. Breakdowns are critical in psytrance — they're where the psychedelic atmospheric elements take over (reversed sounds, granular textures, vocal samples, sci-fi FX), and the re-entry of the kick-bass after a breakdown is the peak moment. Layering: psytrance typically has 3-5 layers of melodic/textural content above the kick-bass engine — acid lines, lead synths (supersaw or FM), atmospheric pads, FX sweeps, and rhythmic percussion. Everything above the bass sits in the mid-to-high frequency range because the bass owns everything below 200Hz. For darkpsy specifically: sound design replaces melody. Granular synthesis, extreme pitch modulation, bit-crushing, ring modulation, and feedback loops create the 'forest' textures. Many darkpsy producers use modular synths or heavy Max/MSP/Reaktor patching for sound generation.

kick.note: Straight 4-on-the-floor at the QUARTER note level (steps 0, 4, 8, 12), but the pattern is notated in 16th notes because the bass fills every 16th. The kick itself is SHORT — punchy transient with minimal tail, leaving room for the bass. Some producers ghost-kick on off-beats at lower velocity for groove variation.

snare.note: No snare in traditional psytrance. The genre deliberately avoids snare/clap backbeats. When percussive accents appear, they're tom-like hits, noise bursts, or synthetic transients placed for tension — never a 2-and-4 snare pattern.

clap.note: Claps are rare in psytrance. When used, they appear in breakdowns or as occasional accents, never as a steady pattern. The genre rejects the house/techno clap-on-4 convention.

ch.note: Closed hi-hats on 8th notes, sitting high in the mix (10kHz+), providing the rhythmic framework above the kick-bass engine. Often layered with a second hat pattern at lower velocity on off-16ths for a shimmering effect. Very short, tight, and filtered — they shouldn't compete with the bass frequencies at all.

oh.note: Open hats on the off-beat 8th notes (between kicks), creating a driving upbeat feel. In more progressive psytrance, these might be replaced with ride cymbal hits or metallic textures.

bass.note: The psytrance bass is a ROLLING 16th-note pattern — every single 16th note has a bass hit, tightly sidechained to duck under each kick. Waveform is typically sawtooth or square run through a resonant low-pass filter with aggressive envelope modulation (high envMod = the filter opens and closes with each note, creating the characteristic 'wah' sound). The notes typically follow a minor scale pattern, often with chromatic movement for tension. In darkpsy, switch to FM synthesis: carrier at the root, modulator ratio at 1:1 to 3:1, with the modulation index automated from 0.5 to 8.0 — this creates the squelchy, metallic, alien bass sounds. Resonance is KEY — at 0.6-0.8, the filter self-oscillates slightly, adding that acidic, singing quality to each note. For the 'rolling' triplet feel, program some bars with 16th-note triplets instead of straight 16ths.

swing.note: Psytrance is DEAD STRAIGHT — no swing whatsoever. The hypnotic, trance-inducing quality comes precisely from the rigid, metronomic pulse. The psychedelic quality comes from the timbral modulation and layered polyrhythms, not from rhythmic swing. Any shuffle or swing immediately destroys the genre's identity.
