modulation.note: Modulation is EVERYTHING in psytrance — it's what makes a static pattern feel alive and psychedelic. The primary modulation target is the bass filter cutoff, typically modulated by an LFO synced to 1/16 or by the amplitude envelope. Secondary targets: FM modulation index (for darkpsy squelches), pitch modulation (slight detuning or octave jumps for accents), and pan automation on mid-frequency elements for stereo width. Long-form modulation is equally important: filter sweeps that evolve over 16-32 bars, slowly opening or closing to build and release tension. Automation of delay feedback, reverb sends, and flanger depth creates the psychedelic 'trip' quality. In darkpsy, modulation becomes extreme — random LFOs, sample-and-hold modulation, chaotic feedback loops.

mixing.note: The kick and bass together form a SINGLE entity that dominates the mix. They should feel like one instrument pumping together. High-pass everything else at 200-300Hz ruthlessly — nothing competes with the bass engine below that point. The mix is deliberately narrow in the low end (mono below 150Hz) and wide in the highs (stereo spread on hats, FX, atmospheres). Master compression should be moderate — psytrance needs dynamics for the sidechain pumping to work. Many psytrance producers use a dedicated sub bus for kick+bass, processed together with gentle bus compression and careful EQ to ensure they interlock. The overall mix should feel relentless and driving but not fatiguing — the bass needs warmth despite its aggression. Reverb is used sparingly on the bass (dry and upfront) but liberally on atmospheric elements (long reverbs, ping-pong delays on FX). For darkpsy, the mix can be more lo-fi and aggressive — less polish, more character, more distortion on the master bus.

psytrance.reasoning: Psytrance is one of the most technically demanding electronic genres to produce because the kick-bass relationship requires surgical precision — millisecond-level phase alignment, exact sidechain timing, and careful frequency carving. It's also one of the most modular: the same fundamental engine (kick + rolling bass + hats) supports a massive spectrum from euphoric full-on to nightmarish darkpsy, simply by changing the timbral character of the bass and the harmonic content of the layers above it. The genre's rejection of traditional song structure elements (no snare backbeat, no chord progressions in the conventional sense, no verse-chorus) means all the musical interest must come from timbral evolution, modulation, and tension/release through arrangement. Understanding the triplet-against-straight polyrhythmic interplay is crucial — this is what gives psytrance its 'rolling' quality that distinguishes it from regular trance. Darkpsy takes this further by removing melody entirely and making sound design the compositional tool — it's closer to musique concrète than to pop music in its approach to sonic material.
references: Dancing Galaxy - Astral Projection (the Goa trance blueprint — melodic, driving, euphoric, with the classic rolling bassline and acid-influenced leads. This is where the genre's DNA was established)

Avalon - Astrix (modern full-on psytrance perfection — the kick-bass relationship is textbook, the breakdown is massive, the energy arc is flawless. Study the bass sound: sawtooth through resonant filter, every 16th note, perfectly sidechained)

Vini Vici - Great Spirit (crossover full-on psytrance that hit mainstream — shows the genre's peak-time energy potential, with the characteristic tribal vocal chop breakdowns and relentless rolling bass)

Shpongle - Divine Moments of Truth (psychedelic downtempo/psytrance hybrid by Simon Posford and Raja Ram — more experimental, showcasing the genre's connection to psychedelic culture and world music influences)

Infected Mushroom - Converting Vegetarians (Israeli psytrance at its most ambitious — long-form tracks with extreme dynamic range, virtuosic sound design, and the signature Infected Mushroom bass sound: aggressive, distorted, constantly morphing)
Kindzadza - Galaxy Drivers (darkpsy masterclass — atonal, chaotic, relentless FM-synthesized bass squelches with zero melody. Pure sound design as composition. The bass sounds like alien machinery)

Psykovsky - Debut (extreme darkpsy — the bass patterns here use rapid-fire FM modulation and pitch bends that create a sense of controlled chaos. Not for the faint of heart, but technically brilliant)

Derango - Forest-style psytrance with organic textures — tribal percussion layers, wooden sound design elements mixed with synthetic bass, representing the 'forest' subgenre's connection to nature imagery

Hallucinogen - LSD (Simon Posford's classic Goa/psytrance track — the acid line in the second half is one of the most iconic in the genre, showing the direct lineage from TB-303 acid to psytrance bass)

Neelix - Leave Me Alone (progressive psytrance — slower, groovier, with a more restrained bass and emphasis on the groove and build rather than maximum intensity. Shows the more accessible end of the spectrum)

uk_garage.name: UK Garage / 2-Step
uk_garage.description: UK Garage — and its most iconic mutation, 2-step — is London's definitive contribution to the rhythmic vocabulary of dance music. Born in the mid-1990s from the collision of American garage house (the soulful, vocal-driven house of New York's Paradise Garage and New Jersey's Club Zanzibar), jungle's rhythmic complexity, R&B's vocal sensibility, and London's pirate radio culture, UKG became the dominant sound of British nightlife from roughly 1996 to 2002 — a golden era of MC-hosted raves, dubplate culture, and massive crossover hits that put underground dance music on Top of the Pops. The genre exists on a spectrum. On one end: speed garage (circa 1995-1997), which took American garage house, sped it up to 130+ BPM, and added heavy, wobbling Reese basslines. Think of DJ EZ's early Tuff Jam dubs, Armand Van Helden's 'Sugar Daddy', Double 99's 'Ripgroove' — four-on-the-floor kicks with shuffled hats and dark, rolling bass. On the other end: 2-step (circa 1997-2001), the revolution. 2-step REMOVED the kick from beat 3. That single absence changed everything. By dropping the kick on the third beat of the bar (and often displacing it to an offbeat position nearby), producers created a 'skippy', lopsided groove that swung harder than any straight four-on-the-floor pattern could. The name '2-step' comes from the shuffling, two-step dance that the syncopated rhythm naturally inspired — you couldn't just nod your head, your whole body had to move to resolve the rhythmic tension. The pioneers of the 2-step sound: MJ Cole (whose 'Sincere' is arguably the genre's most perfectly produced track — crystalline production, pitched-up vocal chops, that SKIP in the kick pattern), Todd Edwards (the New Jersey micro-sampling genius whose chopped-vocal technique became UKG's melodic signature — he'd take R&B acapellas, chop them into 1/16th and 1/32nd note fragments, pitch them up, and reassemble them into new melodies that were simultaneously familiar and alien), El-B (the dark side of 2-step — moody, sub-bass-heavy, minimal, pointing directly toward what would become dubstep), Wookie (whose 'Battle' and remix of Lil' Mo's 'Time After Time' defined the R&B-influenced vocal side), Zed Bias (whose 'Neighbourhood' bridged 2-step and the emerging dubstep sound), Tuff Jam (Karl 'Tuff Enuff' Brown and Matt 'Jam' Lamont — the architects of speed garage who helped the genre evolve into 2-step), and Sunship (Ceri Evans, master of the shuffled beat and pitched vocal). The vocal tradition is crucial: UKG is a VOCAL genre. Where techno strips the voice away, UKG puts it front and center — but processed, chopped, pitched up (+2 to +5 semitones for that signature bright, almost childlike quality), and woven into the rhythm. The MC culture (MC Creed, MC PSG, Sparky, Kie) on pirate stations like Rinse FM, Déjà Vu, and Freek FM was equally important — the MC's rapid-fire chat over the skippy beats was an art form in itself. The scene imploded around 2001-2002 due to a combination of moral panic (the 'garage violence' narrative in the tabloid press), venue shutdowns, and the genre splintering: the dark, bass-heavy side became dubstep (Skream, Benga, Digital Mystikz), the choppy rhythmic side became grime (Wiley's 'Ice Rink' is essentially 2-step with the tempo cranked and the mood turned hostile), and the soulful side went underground or commercial. But UKG's rhythmic DNA lives on in literally everything the UK has produced since — bassline, funky, grime, dubstep, UK drill, even modern pop productions by producers who grew up on pirate radio. The 2-step swing is embedded in British musical DNA.
uk_garage.production: UK Garage production is the art of making complex rhythms feel effortless. The drum programming is everything — specifically, the relationship between where the kick IS and where it ISN'T. In classic 2-step, the kick hits on beat 1 (step 0) with full confidence, establishing the downbeat. Then — crucially — it does NOT hit on beat 3 (step 8). Instead, it might hit on the 'and' of beat 2 (step 6), or the 'e' of beat 3 (step 9), or it might skip beat 3 entirely and not return until beat 4 (step 12). This absence on beat 3 is what creates the 'skip' — your body expects a downbeat and gets air instead, creating a lurch that forces you to move differently than four-on-the-floor allows. The snare/clap pattern reinforces the syncopation: typically on beats 2 and 4 (steps 4 and 12) but often with ghost snares on the 'e' or 'a' of nearby beats, creating a shuffled, almost jazz-like backbeat. Hi-hats are BUSY — continuous 16th notes with heavy swing (55-65%) and pronounced velocity programming. The hat pattern often has accents on offbeats that create a shuffling, ticking quality. Open hats on offbeats add lift and air. The swing percentage is absolutely critical — too little and it sounds stiff, too much and it sounds drunk. UKG lives in a narrow sweet spot around 58-63% where the groove feels like a human drummer with perfect time but natural feel. The bass is a defining element. Speed garage uses the Reese bass — a detuned sawtooth pair (two saws slightly detuned from each other, creating that characteristic 'woozy' beating effect), low-passed but with enough harmonic content to growl and rumble. 2-step bass is typically cleaner and sub-heavier — a deep sine or triangle sub-bass with occasional pitch bends and slides, sitting at 40-60Hz and FELT rather than heard. Some 2-step tracks use no bass notes at all on certain beats, creating bass-frequency negative space that mirrors the kick's skip pattern. The vocal chop technique (pioneered by Todd Edwards, perfected by MJ Cole and Sunship) is UKG's melodic signature: take an R&B vocal, chop it into tiny fragments (32nd notes, 16th notes), pitch them up +2 to +5 semitones, and reassemble them rhythmically so the syllables create new melodic phrases. The pitched-up quality gives UKG vocals their signature bright, euphoric, almost helium-like character. Some producers used whole vocal performances (Craig David's vocals on Artful Dodger's 'Re-Rewind' for instance), but the CHOPPED vocal is the production hallmark. Organ stabs are a classic UKG element — bright, funky organ chords (usually minor 7ths or 9ths) with a fast attack and medium-short decay, placed on offbeats to reinforce the shuffle. These connect UKG to its American garage house roots. Strings and pads provide harmonic bed — lush, warm string pads playing simple chord progressions (often I-vi-IV-V or similar pop-soul changes in minor keys), giving UKG its emotional, soulful quality. The reverb treatment is distinctive: drums are relatively dry (to preserve rhythmic clarity), but vocals and pads swim in warm, medium-length reverb that creates a 'nightclub' atmosphere. The overall production aesthetic is polished and musical — unlike the lo-fi rawness of grime or gqom, UKG production values are HIGH. Clean mixes, careful EQ separation, dynamic vocal processing. This is music that aspires to sound expensive.

kick.note: THE 2-step kick pattern — the skip that defines the genre. Beat 1 (step 0): solid, confident downbeat. Step 6 ('and' of beat 2): a displaced hit that pushes against the grid. Step 10 ('and' of beat 3): the DISPLACED beat 3 — NOT on step 8 where four-on-the-floor would place it, but two 16th notes late, creating the signature lurch. Step 12 (beat 4): re-establishing the pulse. The absence on step 8 is where the 'skip' lives — that hole is the whole genre. The kick itself is clean, tight, round — a house kick with moderate punch, not too boomy (the sub-bass handles low-end weight), not too clicky (this isn't techno). Short-medium decay so it doesn't mask the busy hat pattern. Some tracks vary: kick on just steps 0 and 10 (ultra-sparse), or 0, 4, 10, 14 (different skip pattern). The unifying rule: beat 3 (step 8) is EMPTY or displaced.

snare.note: Clean backbeat on 2 and 4 — the anchor point. While the kick skips and shuffles, the snare is RELIABLE, giving the dancer a consistent reference point. Tight, controlled — a crisp layered snare with body and crack but not too much ring. Often layered with a clap for width. Some productions add ghost snares (very quiet hits on steps 3, 7, 11, 15 — the 16th notes before each backbeat) to add shuffle and swing to the snare pattern itself.

clap.note: Layered with snare on 2 and 4. The clap adds the high-frequency 'crack' while the snare provides body. Together they create a full, satisfying backbeat. Some productions use clap alone (without snare) for a brighter, more percussive feel.

ch.note: Continuous swung 16th notes — the ticking clock of UKG. The swing value (58-63%) is CRITICAL and must be applied to these hats specifically. Velocity programming is essential: accents on certain offbeats (steps 2, 6, 10, 14 slightly louder) create a shuffling lilt. Ghost notes between accents at ~40% velocity. The hats should sound like a very precise human drummer with a jazz feel — mechanical enough to be steady but swung enough to groove. The hat sound itself: thin, crisp, high-passed aggressively (8kHz+), short decay. Some tracks use a shaker layered with or replacing the hat for a softer, more organic top-end texture.

oh.note: Open hats on offbeats add LIFT — the 'tsss' that gives UKG its airy, floating quality above the sub-bass weight below. Placed on offbeat 16ths to reinforce the shuffle. The open hat on step 14 (the 'a' of beat 4) is particularly characteristic — it creates a pickup into the next bar's downbeat that propels the groove forward. Short-medium decay, not washy.

perc.note: Ghost percussion — rimshots, sidesticks, or tambourine hits at low velocity on the 16th notes before each beat. These fill in the rhythmic texture and add to the shuffled, busy quality without dominating. The cumulative effect of hats + open hats + ghost percussion creates UKG's signature 'busy top end' that contrasts with the sparse, skippy kick pattern below.

bass.style: Two main approaches. SPEED GARAGE bass: the Reese — two detuned sawtooth oscillators (detune +7 to +12 cents apart) creating a beating, wobbling effect, low-passed at 300-500Hz with moderate resonance, giving a growling, rolling quality that moves in waves. Think Double 99 'Ripgroove' — that dark, undulating bass that dominates the low-mids. 2-STEP bass: cleaner, sub-focused. A deep sine or triangle wave at 40-60Hz, playing simple patterns that lock with the kick — often mirroring the kick's skip pattern so that bass notes land where kicks do, reinforcing the rhythm with frequency weight. Pitch slides between notes (portamento 20-40ms) add musicality. Occasional octave jumps (+1 octave for a bar) create excitement. The bass often DROPS OUT for a beat or two, creating sub-bass negative space that makes the return hit harder. Some 2-step tracks (El-B style) use a darker, more dubby sub-bass with slight distortion and slow filter movement, pointing toward the dubstep that would follow.
bass.waveform: sine (2-step) or detuned sawtooth pair (speed garage)
bass.note: For 2-step: cutoff low (0.15-0.2), resonance minimal, pure sub-bass territory. For speed garage: cutoff higher (0.3-0.5), resonance at 0.2-0.3 for that growling, resonant Reese character. The Reese bass needs movement — automate the filter cutoff slowly (LFO at 0.2-0.5Hz) so the bass 'breathes' and shifts tonally. For both styles: the bass must lock TIGHT to the kick pattern. In 2-step, the bass often plays on steps 0 and 10 (mirroring the kick's skip pattern), leaving step 8 empty — bass AND kick skip together, making the groove coherent.

targets: hi-hat velocity pattern (subtle humanization)
bass filter cutoff (Reese bass breathing — slow LFO at 0.2-0.5Hz)
vocal chop pitch/formant (per-chop variation)
organ stab filter (brightness variation per hit)
pad filter cutoff (slow, sweeping warmth changes over 8-16 bars)
reverb send levels (pulling elements in and out of space)
bass pitch bend (slides between notes, ±2 semitones)
modulation.envelopes: The swing-feel is UKG's primary 'modulation' — the 58-63% shuffle applied to hi-hats and percussion fundamentally alters the time-feel of every element. Kick: fast attack, short-medium decay (100-150ms), slight pitch envelope down for punch. Snare: fast attack, medium decay (80-120ms). Bass (2-step): fast attack, medium sustain, pitch envelope for slides. Bass (Reese): slow attack (20-40ms for that 'wub' onset), long sustain, no pitch envelope. Vocal chops: variable per-chop — some short and percussive (30ms), some sustained (200ms+), creating rhythmic variety within the vocal phrase. Organ stabs: near-instant attack, 50-100ms decay for that plucky, rhythmic quality. The overall envelope philosophy: SNAPPY on drums and stabs (preserving rhythmic clarity for the complex swing patterns), SMOOTH on bass and pads (providing warm contrast to the busy percussion).

mixing.loudness: Moderate — UKG needs clarity for its complex rhythmic interplay. Target -8 to -6 LUFS. The shuffled hat patterns, ghost percussion, and skippy kick need clean transient definition that over-limiting destroys. The vocal chops need to sparkle above the mix without being squashed. This is polished music with dynamic range — not the crushed loudness of gabber or the raw clipping of gqom.
mixing.stereoWidth: Wide and lush. Kick and bass: mono center, anchoring the groove. Snare/clap: center with slight stereo spread from layering. Hi-hats: subtle stereo variation — slightly different positions for consecutive hits creates a shimmering, head-moving quality. Vocal chops: panned across the stereo field, with different chops at different positions creating a vocal constellation. Pads and strings: wide stereo, using chorus or stereo reverb, creating the warm bed that the drums punch through. Organ stabs: slightly off-center (±20%). The result should feel SPACIOUS — UKG is nightclub music, and the mix should evoke the feeling of a dark room with a massive sound system, bass in your chest and cymbals sparkling above your head.
mixing.eqEmphasis: Balanced with sub-bass weight and high-end sparkle. Sub (30-60Hz): deep and powerful from the bass — this is the physical weight of UKG. Low-mids (100-300Hz): carefully controlled — the kick and bass must not clash here. Surgical EQ to separate kick body (80-150Hz) from bass fundamental (40-60Hz). Mids (500Hz-2kHz): clear and open for vocal chops and organ stabs. Presence (2-5kHz): the vocal chops' intelligibility and the snare's crack live here — keep it crisp. High end (8-16kHz): sparkling from the shuffled hats, open hats, and percussion. The overall tonality is BRIGHT and WARM simultaneously — warm from the sub-bass and pads, bright from the pitched-up vocals and busy hats. UKG should never sound dark or murky — it's a Saturday-night-out sound, polished and inviting.
mixing.compression: Precise and controlled. Bus compression on the drum bus (2:1, fast attack to catch transients, 60-80ms release to let the swing breathe — longer release would flatten the groove). Sidechain the bass from the kick: essential for clarity, ratio 3:1, fast attack, 80-100ms release. The sidechain must be TIGHT — at these tempos with these syncopated patterns, a sloppy sidechain would create rhythmic artifacts. The vocal chops benefit from individual compression (4:1, fast attack) to maintain consistent level across different chop lengths and pitches. Master chain: gentle EQ, light glue compression (1.5:1), transparent limiter. The goal is POLISH — clean, professional, radio-ready. Every element should be clearly audible and occupying its own frequency/stereo space.

uk_garage.reasoning: UK Garage's production logic flows from one revolutionary rhythmic insight: removing the kick from beat 3 transforms a four-on-the-floor house groove into something that swings, skips, and compels the body to move in two-step patterns rather than simple head-nodding. This 'skip' on beat 3 is the single most important production decision in the genre's history, and everything else flows from it. The kick on beat 1 (step 0) establishes the downbeat — you know where you are. The ABSENCE on beat 3 (step 8) creates expectation violation — your brain predicted a kick, got silence, and your body compensates by shifting weight. The displaced kick that appears nearby (step 6, 9, or 10 depending on the pattern) resolves the tension but in an unexpected position, creating the 'lurch' that IS the 2-step dance. This rhythmic principle draws from both jungle (which fractured breakbeats into complex polyrhythmic patterns at 160-170 BPM) and R&B/hip-hop (which used laid-back, swung grooves at 80-100 BPM). UKG sits at exactly DOUBLE the hip-hop tempo (130-140 BPM = 65-70 BPM half-time), which means the shuffled, skippy feel is simultaneously a fast dance groove AND a slow, head-nodding half-time groove depending on which metric level you lock to. This dual-tempo quality is why UKG can be both euphoric (read at full speed) and sensual (read at half-time) — it serves both the rave and the slow wine. The swing percentage (58-63%) is calibrated to this duality: enough shuffle to feel human and groovy (like a hip-hop beat at half-time) but not so much that it loses the forward drive needed at 135 BPM. The busy hi-hat pattern (swung 16ths with velocity accents) serves a critical function: it provides the fine-grained temporal grid against which the kick's skip becomes audible and meaningful. Without the constant ticking hats, the missing kick on beat 3 would just sound like empty space. With the hats, it sounds like a CHOICE — the groove is clearly established and the kick is clearly absent, creating intentional tension. The pitched-up vocal chops (+2 to +5 semitones) serve multiple purposes: (1) they brighten the vocal, moving it out of the frequency range where it would compete with the bass and kick; (2) they create the euphoric, youthful quality that gives UKG its Saturday-night energy; (3) they allow producers to create new melodic content from existing vocal material — a chopped syllable at +3 semitones becomes a completely different melodic note than the same syllable at +5 semitones, enabling melodic sequencing from a single vocal source. Todd Edwards' micro-sampling technique is the apex of this approach — he created entire songs from dozens of chopped vocal fragments, each pitched and placed to create coherent melodies and harmonies from chaos. The sub-bass weight (deep sine at 40-60Hz) connects UKG to its jungle/drum & bass heritage — London sound system culture demands physical bass weight. But where jungle's bass was often a distorted, aggressive Reese, 2-step's bass is CLEAN and ROUND — a pure sub that you feel in your sternum without it cluttering the mid-range where the vocal chops and organ stabs need to operate. The Reese bass of speed garage (the earlier UKG variant) was more aggressive, reflecting the genre's closer proximity to jungle; as UKG evolved into 2-step, the bass cleaned up, reflecting the move toward R&B-influenced polish. The organ stabs connect UKG to its American garage house roots — the sound of Larry Levan's Paradise Garage filtered through London's pirate radio. The lush pads and string arrangements reflect UKG's aspiration to MUSICALITY — unlike minimal techno or grime, UKG wanted to be beautiful, emotional, and musically sophisticated while still being underground dance music. This tension between underground credibility and pop musicality defined UKG's cultural moment and ultimately contributed to its fragmentation: the pop side became Artful Dodger and Craig David (mainstream success); the dark side became dubstep and grime (underground innovation); and the middle ground — the perfectly balanced 2-step of MJ Cole and Wookie — became a lost golden age that UK producers have been trying to recapture ever since.
references: Sincere - MJ Cole (1998, the 2-step masterpiece — listen to how the kick skips beat 3, how the pitched-up vocal chops create melody from fragments, how the sub-bass locks to the skip pattern. Every element of this track is a production textbook)

Saved My Life / Never Felt Like This Before - Todd Edwards (the micro-sampling godfather — entire vocal arrangements built from dozens of chopped, pitched fragments of R&B records. The technique that defined UKG's melodic identity)

Re-Rewind (The Crowd Say Bo Selecta) - Artful Dodger ft. Craig David (1999, UKG's biggest crossover hit — the skippy beat, the 2-step swing, Craig David's smooth vocal locked to the rhythm. This put garage on Top of the Pops)

Ripgroove - Double 99 (1997, speed garage anthem — the dark, rolling Reese bass over a four-on-the-floor kick with shuffled hats. The bass IS the track. This is the speed garage side of UKG before 2-step emerged)
Battle - Wookie (2000, from the debut album — deep, moody 2-step with a subby bassline and chopped female vocal. Shows the R&B-influenced emotional depth of 2-step at its best)

Neighbourhood - Zed Bias (2000, on Locked On — dark, minimal 2-step that points directly toward dubstep. The bass is heavier, the mood is darker, the skip pattern is sparser. The bridge track between UKG and what came next)

Almighty Father - El-B (on Ghost Records — the dark prophet of 2-step. El-B's productions stripped UKG back to its skeletal essence: sub-bass, skippy kicks, minimal percussion, and MOOD. The direct ancestor of dubstep's half-step patterns)
Flowers - Sunship ft. Warrior Queen (classic UKG vocal production — Ceri Evans' shuffled beats with pitched vocal chops and garage organ stabs. Pure London dancefloor energy)

Gotta Get Thru This - Daniel Bedingfield (2001, lo-fi bedroom 2-step that became a massive pop hit — recorded in his bedroom, the raw production became part of the charm. Shows UKG's DIY spirit alongside its polished mainstream face)

Ice Rink / Eskimo - Wiley (2001-2002, the genesis of grime FROM 2-step — Wiley took the skippy rhythm, cranked the tempo, replaced the soulful vocals with aggressive MC bars, and created grime. These tracks are the exact moment of mutation)
RIP Groove - Double 99 (Tim Deluxe remix) (2001, showing how the 2-step sound evolved — Tim Deluxe's remix adds jazzy chords and a tighter swing to the original speed garage banger)

Lucky Star - Basement Jaxx (1999, UK garage filtered through Basement Jaxx's eclectic sensibility — the Latin percussion and disco samples over a 2-step rhythm show the genre's range beyond its London core)

breakcore.name: Breakcore
breakcore.description: Breakcore emerged from the collision of jungle, hardcore techno, and industrial music in the 1990s. It is defined by extreme break manipulation, where the Amen break and other classic breaks are chopped, time-stretched, and reconstructed beyond recognition. The genre features polyrhythmic complexity with multiple rhythmic layers operating at different tempos, genre-crossing sample collages drawing from classical music to death metal to children's TV, intentional chaos that challenges conventional song structure, and timbral violence through harsh digital distortion and bit-crushing.
breakcore.production: Production centers on micro-editing individual transients, applying complex groove templates to chopped breaks, and polyrhythmic layering across subdivisions. Arrangement rejects traditional verse/chorus formats in favor of organized disorder with rapid section changes. Essential tools include tracker-based sequencers (Renoise, FastTracker), Ableton Live for real-time warping, and hardware samplers. Every frequency fights for space simultaneously, with dynamic extremes from whisper-quiet breakdowns to maximum-volume chaos.
references: Come On My Selector - Squarepusher
Windowlicker - Aphex Twin
Hajnal - Venetian Snares
Addicted to You - Alec Empire
Tout Petit Moineau - Igorrr
breakcore.lineage: Emerged from the collision of jungle/drum & bass, hardcore techno/gabber, and industrial/noise in the mid-1990s, codified by Squarepusher, Aphex Twin, and Alec Empire.
breakcore.currentScene: Active underground with bedroom producer accessibility via software democratization, integration with internet/meme culture, cross-pollination with dubstep and trap. Contemporary artists like Sewerslvt and Ruby My Dear push the genre on Bandcamp and SoundCloud.

complextro.name: Complextro
complextro.description: Complextro fuses electro house foundations with glitch hop, dubstep, and chiptune influences, creating ADD-friendly electronic music matching video game aesthetics. Coined by Porter Robinson around 2010. Core characteristics include aggressive sawtooth bass with heavy distortion, 8-bit inspired arpeggios and square wave textures, sharp cuts and stutters creating digital glitch melodies, and multiple melodic lines weaving in and out. Heavy use of sus2, add9, and minor7 chords for nostalgic quality.
complextro.production: Targets -8 to -6 LUFS with aggressive loudness for club systems. Steep high-pass at 30-40Hz, aggressive midrange scooping at 300-500Hz, presence boost at 1-3kHz. Bus compression at 4:1-6:1 with fast attack. Multiple distortion stages: tape saturation, bit crushers, and tube emulation. Delays feature dotted 8th and 16th note patterns with filtering.
references: Language - Porter Robinson
Grand Theft Ecstasy - Feed Me
Internet Friends - Knife Party
Icarus - Madeon
Scary Monsters and Nice Sprites - Skrillex
complextro.lineage: Born in the late 2000s fusing electro house with glitch hop, dubstep, and chiptune, peaking 2010-2012 before its techniques were absorbed into broader bass music and future bass.
complextro.currentScene: Peak creative period has passed but legacy persists through nostalgia cycles. Original techniques now standard in modern dubstep and trap production.

drift_phonk.name: Drift Phonk
references: Close Eyes - DVRST
Metamorphosis - INTERWORLD
Murder In My Mind - Kordhell
Scary Garry - Kaito Shoma
RAVE - DXRK
drift_phonk.description: Drift phonk is the aggressive evolution of traditional phonk music, characterized by TR-808 cowbell patterns, aggressive distorted basslines, and slowed Memphis rap samples. The genre emerged from the intersection of Memphis rap heritage and Eastern European drift/racing culture, exploding virally through TikTok between 2020-2022. Core elements include rapid-fire heavily compressed cowbell sequences (the genre's signature), pitch-shifted rap samples, deep distorted 808 kicks with extreme sidechain compression, and minimalist loop-based composition.
drift_phonk.production: Loop-centric arrangement with 8-16 bar loops and minimal variation, designed for instant gratification with hooks within the first 5 seconds for social media. Mixing prioritizes low-end dominance with 808 kicks occupying 40-100Hz, everything else high-passed above 120Hz, and extreme sidechain compression. Cowbells centered around 800Hz-2kHz with heavy saturation. Mastering targets -6 to -8 LUFS with brick wall limiting. Vocal manipulation involves slowing Memphis rap acapellas to 66-75% speed with pitch shifting down 3-5 semitones.
drift_phonk.lineage: Rooted in 1990s Memphis rap (Three 6 Mafia, DJ Screw) and the 2010s phonk revival, drift phonk mutated through Eastern European producers (2018-2020) who combined traditional phonk with harder electronic influences and automotive culture.
drift_phonk.currentScene: Globally distributed with strongest roots in Russia/Eastern Europe, viral on TikTok and YouTube, with growing Brazilian funk automotivo crossover. Active artists include Kordhell, Interworld, and DXRK.

future_garage.name: Future Garage
future_garage.description: Future garage emerged in mid-2000s London as a natural evolution from UK garage's shuffled rhythms, absorbing atmospheric depth from early dubstep while maintaining garage's syncopated swing. Core elements include shuffled garage rhythm patterns with syncopated hi-hats and displaced snare, ethereal reverb-drenched atmospherics, emotional jazz-influenced chord progressions, detailed percussion with ghost notes and polyrhythms, and sub-bass emphasis providing weight without overwhelming mids.
future_garage.production: Extended 32-64 bar introductions build atmosphere before progressive layering. Mix features wide stereo imaging, controlled low-end, mid-range clarity, and high-frequency air. Swing timing not quantized to grid, maintaining human groove with velocity variation. Automation-heavy with constant movement in filtering and reverb sends. Conservative mastering preserving dynamic range.
references: Archangel - Burial
Serised - Mount Kimbie
CMYK - James Blake
Acceptance - Synkro
Afterglow - Phaeleh
future_garage.lineage: Crystallized around 2007-2009 in London when producers stripped UK garage's R&B vocals while retaining shuffled percussion, merging with early dubstep's introspective depth.
future_garage.currentScene: London remains the creative center alongside Pacific Northwest and Berlin scenes, supported by Hotflush and R&S Records.

gym_phonk.name: Gym Phonk
gym_phonk.description: Gym phonk represents the evolution of traditional phonk toward workout optimization, maintaining the genre's dark aesthetic while emphasizing motivational energy and physical intensity. The sweet spot sits at 135-140 BPM, optimal for HIIT while steady enough for strength training. Core elements include four-on-the-floor kick emphasis with syncopated accents, extended sustain 808s with aggressive attack, driving 16th note hi-hat patterns, and reinforced snares layered to cut through ambient gym noise.
gym_phonk.production: Frequency management optimized for gym acoustics: controlled sub-bass to avoid room resonance, clear bass definition, scooped midrange, enhanced 2-8kHz presence for cutting through ambient noise. Targets -6 to -8 LUFS for competitive gym playlist volumes. Arrangement features strategic motivational peaks with vocal samples timed for maximum psychological impact.
references: Beast Mode - GHOSTEMANE x Phonk Killer
Pump Iron - DJ Sacred x Memphis Cult
Deadlift Dreams - Phonk Factory
gym_phonk.lineage: Evolved from Memphis rap through traditional phonk to workout-optimized production, catalyzed by TikTok fitness content creating demand for energy-optimized phonk.
gym_phonk.currentScene: Primarily US-based with growing scenes in Russia/Eastern Europe and Brazil, distributed via fitness influencer culture on TikTok and YouTube.

jersey_club.name: Jersey Club
jersey_club.description: Jersey club emerged from Newark, New Jersey around 1999-2000. Pioneer DJ Tameil introduced Baltimore club records to Newark's teen party scene, and the Brick Bandits crew established the sound by fusing Baltimore's uptempo house/hip-hop hybrid with harder, more aggressive regional characteristics. Core elements include triplet kick patterns creating a heavy 'bouncy' groove, extensively fragmented rap and R&B vocals reconstructed into percussive elements, aggressive punchy kicks, deep 808 sub-bass, and sharp staccato sample chops.
jersey_club.production: Sony Acid Pro is the genre standard DAW. Production involves chopping source material to smallest rhythmic units with precise grid-snapped timing. Mixing prioritizes loudness with compressed aggressive mixes for club playback, mid-range clarity for vocals and snares cutting through dense low-end. Mastering uses heavy limiting with multiband compression.
references: Trey Songz Na Na Remix - DJ Sliink
Vibe - Cookiee Kawaii
Just Wanna Rock - Lil Uzi Vert
Patty Cake - Jayhood
Microdosing - Uniiqu3
jersey_club.lineage: Evolved from Baltimore club's uptempo house/hip-hop hybrid, filtered through Newark's existing house music foundation, spreading to surrounding New Jersey cities by 2005.
jersey_club.currentScene: Achieved mainstream recognition with major artists incorporating elements (Drake, Lil Uzi Vert), received an official New Jersey historical marker in 2025, with global expansion through TikTok.

neurofunk.name: Neurofunk
neurofunk.description: Neurofunk emerged from the darker, more technical corners of drum & bass in the mid-1990s. The defining element is hyper-complex modulated basslines that seem to 'think' and evolve, with multiple layers of sub-bass, mid-bass, and upper harmonics processed independently through constant filter sweeps, distortion modulation, and frequency manipulation. The 'neurofunk' name reflects how basslines function like neural networks with multiple interconnected parameters creating emergent complexity.
neurofunk.production: Bass synthesis uses a multi-layer approach: pure sine/triangle sub-bass, complex filtered/modulated mid-bass, high-frequency articulation through distortion and FM synthesis, and intricate movement with multiple LFOs at different rates. Mixing demands surgical frequency allocation with sub-bass mono and centered, complex EQ curves for bass modulation, and pristine high-end. Phase manipulation via all-pass filters creates stereo width without mono compatibility issues.
references: Wormhole - Ed Rush & Optical
Stigma - Noisia
Universal Truth - Photek
The Blind Prophet - Mefjus
System - Phace & Misanthrop
neurofunk.lineage: Developed from the darker side of 1990s drum & bass, pioneered by Photek and Grooverider, defined by Ed Rush & Optical, and perfected by Noisia's hyper-detailed bass approach.
neurofunk.currentScene: The Netherlands leads innovation (Noisia, Mefjus) with strong UK and German scenes. Current innovation includes hybrid analog/digital synthesis, modular integration, and AI-assisted parameter modulation.

pluggnb.name: Pluggnb
references: Down Like That - Che Ecru
Nowadays - Dro Kenji
Trying - Midwxst
Stick Around - Summrs
If Looks Could Kill - Destroy Lonely
pluggnb.description: Pluggnb blends R&B's ethereal melodic sensibilities with Plugg's rhythmic foundation. Key characteristics include the signature 'plugg guitar' (Roland D-50 'Fantasia' preset with heavy reverb and chorus), heavily auto-tuned vocals, 3-5 layer vocal harmonies, 808-heavy drum programming with trap-influenced hi-hat patterns, and a double-time feel (130-150 BPM feeling like 65-75). Harmonic language emphasizes minor 7th and add9 chords with extended harmony borrowed from neo-soul.
pluggnb.production: Vocal chain: auto-tune, de-esser, EQ with high-shelf boost, slow-attack compressor, large hall reverb, filtered 1/8 note delay, harmonic saturation. 808 design layers sub-bass sine, harmonic content, and transient with subtle pitch drops over 200-500ms. Arrangement innovations include extended 16-32 bar intros, blurred verse/chorus sections, and long instrumental outros. Master bus uses subtle tape saturation and transparent limiting.
pluggnb.lineage: Crystallized around 2018-2020 from the Plugg movement (MexikoDro, StoopidXool) merging with alternative R&B's atmospheric territory (Brent Faiyaz, Summer Walker).
pluggnb.currentScene: Internet-native and growing, with Atlanta emphasizing harder trap drums, LA focusing on vocal production, and a global bedroom producer community on SoundCloud and YouTube.

rawstyle.name: Rawstyle
rawstyle.description: Rawstyle emerged in the early 2010s as a direct response to the increasingly euphoric and commercial direction of mainstream hardstyle. The defining element is the kick: heavily distorted, crushed, and saturated with aggressive mid-range content, longer decay creating a 'crushing' effect. Harmonic content is minimal with single-note stabs, dissonant intervals, and industrial metallic textures. Anti-drop philosophy where drops are often more minimal than builds.
rawstyle.production: Kick synthesis uses multi-layer approach: low-end sine (50-80Hz), heavily saturated mid punch (200-800Hz), extreme-compression click (2-8kHz), and distorted tail extending 200-400ms. Unlike euphoric hardstyle's clean frequency separation, rawstyle embraces controlled chaos with mid-range deliberately overloaded. Arrangement uses anti-buildup philosophy with false drops and relentless pacing.
references: Brutal 6.0 - Radical Redemption
FTP - Warface
Dismissed - Delete
Command & Conquer - Radical Redemption
Machine Gun - Warface
rawstyle.lineage: Emerged around 2010-2012 as a reaction against hardstyle's commercial direction, drawing from hardcore techno, gabber, and industrial to reclaim early hardstyle's aggressive edge.
rawstyle.currentScene: Commands dedicated stages at major festivals (Defqon.1, Qlimax). The Netherlands and Italy remain primary centers, with expansion into Australia, Chile, and Russia.

sigilkore.name: Sigilkore
sigilkore.description: Sigilkore represents a convergence of breakcore aggression with occult mysticism, functioning as both dancefloor destroyer and ritualistic sonic practice. Combines breakcore's polyrhythmic complexity with esoteric samples and dark ambient interludes. Core elements include hyperstutter drum programming, minor modes (often Phrygian or Locrian), deep sustained drones, heavily distorted mid-bass filtered through occult speech formants, and chopped Amen breaks. Hi-hats programmed using prime number sequences.
sigilkore.production: Targets -8 to -12 LUFS preserving rhythmic complexity. Polyrhythmic programming follows mathematical principles from mystical traditions: golden ratio divisions, Fibonacci sequences, occult numerology for structural organization. Multiple algorithmic reverbs create ritualistic spaces. Arrangement follows ritualistic logic: invocation, rising energy, peak chaos, mystical interludes, ritual completion.
references: MXLLY #SIGILKORE - gazecloud
sigilkore.lineage: Emerged from proto-breakcore producers incorporating occult elements around 2019-2020, solidifying by 2021-2022 from crossover between breakcore, hyperpop, digicore, and internet occultism.
sigilkore.currentScene: Self-released on Bandcamp and SoundCloud with DIY ethics, notable in Vancouver and US East Coast scenes. Strong crossover with digicore, hyperpop, and dark ambient.

stutterhouse.name: Stutterhouse
stutterhouse.description: Stutterhouse emerged around 2020 from the intersection of minimal tech house and granular synthesis experimentation. Core characteristics include an unwavering four-on-floor kick foundation, granular synthesis stuttering effects with short 50-200ms audio fragments repeated and manipulated, heavily fragmented vocal chops losing semantic meaning but maintaining rhythmic precision, and complex polyrhythmic percussion layers. Maintains dancefloor functionality despite textural complexity.
stutterhouse.production: Granular processing uses buffer-based capture with 50-200ms grains, 50-80% overlap, and 10-30% position randomization. Vocal fragmentation isolates syllables from 2-4 word phrases and quantizes to subdivision grids. Typical 8-minute arrangements progress from kick+hi-hat through graduated stutter introduction to full polyrhythmic complexity. Mixing high-passes stutter elements above 100-150Hz with mono low-end.
references: Fragmented/004 - Berlin Underground Collective
Stutter.Pattern.Repeat - Digital Minimalism
Granular.Space.Function - Rheinzink
stutterhouse.lineage: Developed from tech house's four-on-floor foundation filtered through glitch music's digital manipulation and minimal techno's stripped-down arrangements, emerging in underground Berlin clubs around 2020.
stutterhouse.currentScene: Centered in Berlin (://about blank, OHM) and London (Fabric Room 3, Dalston), with ~200 active producers in Berlin and ~150 in London.

wave.name: Wave
wave.description: Wave emerged from the intersection of trap's rhythmic foundation and future bass's melodic sensibilities around 2012-2013, crystallizing during 2014-2016 around artists like Lorn, Holy Other, and Shlohmo. Characterized by atmospheric pads with slow attack and long decay, emotional minor-key chord progressions with extended chords, half-time trap rhythms at 140-160 BPM feeling like 70-80, 808 kicks with long sub-bass tails, and cinematic builds with extended 32-64 bar build-ups. Arrangement focused on emotional journey over dancefloor functionality.
wave.production: Atmospheric pads use saw/square waves with heavy unison detune processed through high-pass, chorus, long reverb, and tape saturation. Spatial processing employs multiple reverb sends (hall, plate, spring) with depth creation through near-field leads and far-field atmospherics. Mixing uses gentle multiband compression, 1/4 note sidechain from 808 kick, and conservative limiting preserving dynamic range.
references: Acid Rain - Lorn
Know Where - Holy Other
Wen Uuu - Shlohmo
About You - XXYYXX
I'm God - Clams Casino
wave.lineage: Emerged around 2012-2013 from trap's rhythmic foundation meeting future bass's melodic sensibilities, with SoundCloud enabling rapid scene development.
wave.currentScene: SoundCloud remains the original home. The genre has integrated into broader 'emotional trap' and 'cinematic bass' movements, with revival through TikTok and influence on contemporary hip-hop production.

jeff_mills.name: Jeff Mills
jeff_mills.description: Jeff Mills' 909 sessions are raw, hypnotic, minimal Detroit techno improvisations performed live on a Roland TR-909. Characterized by relentless forward momentum, surgical precision, and subtle variation within repetition.
jeff_mills.philosophy: TENSION through SUBTRACTION and ADDITION. Build through removal to create tension. Hypnotic repetition—lock a groove for 16-64 bars before changing. Micro-variations—change one element at a time (hat velocity, kick placement). No fills for fills' sake—every change serves the groove. Polyrhythmic layering—stack 3-against-4 or 5-against-4 patterns subtly.

kick.notes: Four-on-the-floor foundation, but often drops kicks out for tension. Ghost kicks on off-beats.

snare.notes: Backbeat but often stripped out entirely for long stretches. Ghost notes at lower velocity on 16th subdivisions.

clap.notes: Sometimes layered with snare, sometimes alternating.

ch.notes: Driving 16ths or 8ths, velocity varies subtly (85-127 range). Mills constantly rides the patterns.

oh.notes: Sparse, on off-beats (the and of 2 and 4).

rimshot.notes: Syncopated accents on the e or a of beats.

crash.notes: Very sparse, used for transitions or to mark phrase boundaries.

driving_lock.name: The Driving Lock
driving_lock.description: Full groove with snare and open hats

stripped_tension.name: The Stripped Tension
stripped_tension.description: Single kick, driving hats and ride

tribal_build.name: The Tribal Build
tribal_build.description: Syncopated kick with toms and rimshot

programmingPrinciples: Velocity is expression: Hats should have subtle velocity variation (85-127 range)
Space is sacred: Don't fill every slot; silence creates groove
Evolve slowly: Change 1-2 elements every 8-16 bars maximum
The kick is the anchor: Even when absent, its return defines the drop
Layer textures: Combine metallic (hats/ride) with organic (toms) with punch (kick/snare)
Probability over randomness: Elements should have weighted likelihood, not chaos
references: The Bells
Phase 4
The Jungle
Sonic Destroyer
Changes of Life
